cases.query: How should I choose between classic machine learning, LLM prompting, RAG, and agents for different product situations?

expected.heading: Choosing an Approach
expected.text: The first engineering decision is usually not which model — it is which class of solution:

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Reach for ML when the decision boundary is fuzzy, the signal is distributed across many weak features, or the rules would be brittle and expensive to maintain.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Evaluation is how you measure whether an LLM application is doing the right thing: answer quality, grounding, safety, and regressions over time.

cases.referenceAnswer: Choose the solution class based on the problem shape, not the model brand. For stable rules and tightly controlled error costs, rules or heuristics are the best fit; for labeled data and an explicit prediction target, classic ML is appropriate; for open-ended language tasks without training data, prompting an LLM is a good fit; and for LLMs that need current or private knowledge, RAG is the right choice [[11 AI & ML#Choosing an Approach]]. For multi-step LLM systems, evaluation needs to cover answer quality, grounding, safety, and regressions over time [[11 AI & ML#Intro]].

cases.query: What does a reliable machine learning pipeline look like from data collection through deployment and monitoring?

expected.heading: Generic pipeline
expected.text: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Data collection] --> B[Data cleaning] B --> C[Feature engineering] C --> D[Train test split] D --> E[Model training] E --> F[Evaluation] F --> G[Hyperparameter tuning] G -->

expected.heading: Data Collection and Labeling
expected.text: Define the prediction target first, then collect inputs that are available at inference time and representative of production traffic.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: most of the real work is not the algorithm, it is building a reliable pipeline that is testable, deployable, and monitorable.

cases.referenceAnswer: A reliable ML pipeline starts with defining the prediction target, then collecting inputs that will be available at inference time and representative of production traffic [[Machine Learning#Data Collection and Labeling]]. From there, the workflow typically includes data cleaning, feature engineering, train-test split, model training, evaluation, tuning, deployment, inference, and monitoring [[Machine Learning#Generic pipeline]]. The key engineering challenge is not just the algorithm itself, but building a pipeline that is testable, deployable, and monitorable [[Machine Learning#Intro]].

cases.query: Why is evaluation especially important for LLM applications, and what kinds of signals should it include?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Because LLM output is probabilistic and open-ended, you cannot rely on a single pass/fail assertion

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Data quality, evaluation discipline, guardrails, and monitoring decide whether a system works in production,

expected.heading: Map of the Section
expected.text: - **[[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning|Machine Learning]]** — the classic discipline: training pipelines, [[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/Machine Learning/Types/Type

cases.referenceAnswer: Evaluation matters for LLM applications because their outputs are probabilistic and open-ended, so a single pass/fail assertion is not enough [[11 AI & ML#Intro]]. Instead, evaluation should be layered and include cheap hard checks, scalable semantic judges, fixed regression sets, and production signals [[11 AI & ML#Intro]]. This is especially important because data quality, evaluation discipline, guardrails, and monitoring determine whether the system works in production [[11 AI & ML#Intro]].

cases.query: What are the main correctness risks with C# value types and reference types, and how can they be mitigated?

expected.heading: How It Works
expected.text: assigning a value type copies the value

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Assuming reference types are always safe to pass around can create hidden shared-mutation bugs.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: That nuance matters for correctness, allocations, and API design,

cases.referenceAnswer: C# value types copy their value on assignment, while reference types copy a reference to the same object, so mutation can be shared across aliases [[Types#How It Works]]. That creates hidden shared-mutation bugs when multiple references point to one mutable object, and it is often mitigated by preferring immutability or cloning at ownership boundaries [[Types#Pitfalls]]. Large or mutable structs can also cause correctness problems because you may mutate a copy rather than the original value [[Types#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: What should you do when the context window is getting full, and why is truncation risky?

expected.heading: The Context Budget
expected.text: Everything competes for the same window

expected.heading: Techniques
expected.text: **Ordering and positioning.** Place the most important evidence at the start and end of the context to exploit primacy and recency; bury nothing critical in the middle.

cases.referenceAnswer: When the context window is getting full, you should treat it like a memory budget and decide what to cut rather than letting the runtime truncate arbitrarily. The guidance is to reserve headroom for the output and to compact older history before it crowds out the task. Arbitrary truncation is risky because it usually drops the oldest, and often most important, instructions. [[Context Engineering#The Context Budget]] [[Context Engineering#Techniques]]

cases.query: How do prompt engineering and context engineering differ, and how are they related?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Context engineering is the practice of deliberately deciding what goes into the model's context window

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Prompt engineering is the practice of turning a vague user intention into a precise model task.

cases.referenceAnswer: Prompt engineering is about turning a vague user intention into a precise model task, while context engineering is about deliberately deciding what goes into the model's context window and in what order. The relationship is that context engineering is the broader discipline prompting grows into once a system includes retrieval, tools, and memory. In that setting, the prompt is no longer just a single authored string but an assembled payload. [[Context Engineering#Intro]] [[Prompting#Intro]]

cases.query: What are the main elements of an effective prompt, and what does each one do?

expected.heading: Prompt Anatomy
expected.text: Most effective prompts combine four elements:

cases.referenceAnswer: An effective prompt combines instruction, context, input data, and an output indicator. The instruction narrows behavior, the context biases interpretation, the input anchors the specific case, and the output indicator constrains the format of the answer. Together, these elements reduce uncertainty and make the task more precise. [[Prompting#Prompt Anatomy]]

cases.query: How do generation settings differ from grounding, and what does grounding require the model to do?

expected.heading: LLM Settings
expected.text: Prompt text controls intent, while generation settings control sampling behavior and output boundaries.

expected.heading: Grounding and Citations
expected.text: Grounding constrains model output to evidence from provided context rather than parametric memory.

cases.referenceAnswer: Generation settings control how the model samples and how long or bounded the output can be, while grounding controls what evidence the output is allowed to use. Grounding requires the model to use only the provided sources, attach a citation to each factual claim, abstain if evidence is insufficient or conflicting, and not combine source material with parametric knowledge. In other words, settings shape the output process, but grounding constrains the factual basis of the answer. [[Prompting#LLM Settings]] [[Generation#Grounding and Citations]]

cases.query: How do dense embeddings and BM25 differ in retrieval, and when would you combine them in a RAG system?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The goal is to balance recall (find everything relevant), precision (exclude everything irrelevant), and latency across query types: semantic paraphrases, exact identifiers, and multi-constraint reque

expected.heading: Dense Retrieval — Vector Search
expected.text: An embedding model converts the query and each document chunk into fixed-size vectors in the same space.

expected.heading: Sparse Retrieval — Keyword Search (BM25)
expected.text: BM25 is the same core idea — it is the algorithm behind those search indexes.

cases.referenceAnswer: Dense retrieval uses embeddings to match semantically similar text in vector space, while BM25 matches exact words and ranks documents by term distinctiveness [[Retrieval#Dense Retrieval — Vector Search]] [[Retrieval#Sparse Retrieval — Keyword Search (BM25)]]. In a RAG system, the choice depends on the query type: semantic paraphrases favor vectors, exact identifiers favor keyword search, and multi-constraint requests often need both [[Retrieval#Intro]]. The overall goal is to balance recall, precision, and latency across those cases [[Retrieval#Intro]].

cases.query: Why does monitoring need to observe each RAG stage separately, and what metrics can reveal retrieval problems early?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Each stage can degrade independently.

expected.heading: Instrumentation
expected.text: Each pipeline stage — query translation, embedding, retrieval, reranking, context assembly, generation — should emit its own span within a parent trace.

expected.heading: Deterministic Metrics
expected.text: Compute these on every request — they are free and instant.

cases.referenceAnswer: Monitoring needs stage-level visibility because query translation, embedding, retrieval, reranking, context assembly, and generation can each fail independently [[Monitoring#Intro]] [[Monitoring#Instrumentation]]. Instrumenting each stage with its own span gives per-stage latency breakdown and error attribution [[Monitoring#Instrumentation]]. Early retrieval issues can show up in deterministic metrics like empty-result rate and retrieval count distribution [[Monitoring#Deterministic Metrics]].

cases.query: What are the tradeoffs of using higher-dimensional embeddings, and how can Matryoshka training help reduce cost?

expected.heading: Model Families
expected.text: Three categories dominate production RAG systems: **Proprietary API models** — OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` (1536-dim) and `text-embedding-3-large` (3072-dim) are the most widely deployed.

expected.heading: Dimensionality
expected.text: Higher dimensions give the model more room to represent fine-grained distinctions.

cases.referenceAnswer: Higher-dimensional embeddings can represent finer distinctions, but they also increase storage, compute, and ANN index memory costs [[Embeddings#Dimensionality]]. Matryoshka Representation Learning helps by making any prefix of the full vector meaningful, so you can index shorter vectors for faster search and reserve full dimensions for cases that need more precision [[Embeddings#Dimensionality]]. In practice, this lets systems trade some vector length for lower cost without giving up the option to use the full embedding when needed [[Embeddings#Dimensionality]] [[Embeddings#Model Families]].

cases.query: How does the embedding model fit into the retrieval pipeline from query to ranked candidates?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: At query time, the system embeds the query with the same model, then finds the nearest stored vectors using cosine similarity or dot product.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The user query is converted into one or more search representations — a vector (for semantic search), a set of weighted terms (for keyword search), or both.

expected.heading: Dense Retrieval — Vector Search
expected.text: Chunk vectors are pre-computed and stored at index time; only the query vector is computed at search time.

cases.referenceAnswer: The query is converted into a search representation, often a vector for semantic search, and the same embedding model is used to encode both the query and the indexed chunks [[Retrieval#Intro]] [[Retrieval#Dense Retrieval — Vector Search]]. Chunk vectors are precomputed at index time, while the query vector is computed at search time and compared to stored vectors using similarity search [[Retrieval#Dense Retrieval — Vector Search]] [[Embeddings#Intro]]. The nearest stored vectors become the most relevant candidates for downstream ranking [[Embeddings#Intro]].

cases.query: What are the main stages of training a modern LLM, and how does each stage change what the model is good at?

expected.heading: How LLMs Are Built
expected.text: Training a modern LLM is a three-stage pipeline.

cases.referenceAnswer: A modern LLM is trained in three stages: pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning from human feedback. Pretraining learns next-token prediction over very large text corpora and produces a base model that is good at plausible continuation, while supervised fine-tuning teaches it to behave like an assistant and answer questions rather than just continue text. RLHF then further aligns the model with human preferences and desired behavior [[LLM#How LLMs Are Built]].

cases.query: When should you use prompting, retrieval-augmented generation, or fine-tuning to adapt an LLM to a task?

expected.heading: Adapting an LLM to a Task
expected.text: Three adaptation levers, ordered by cost.

cases.referenceAnswer: The note recommends three adaptation levers in order of cost: prompting, retrieval-augmented generation, and fine-tuning. Prompting is the cheapest and fastest option when you can steer behavior through instructions and examples, RAG is the right choice when facts change quickly or answers must cite sources, and fine-tuning is for task-specific behavior when you need consistent format, tone, or policy [[LLM#Adapting an LLM to a Task]].

cases.query: How do cosine similarity, dot product, and Euclidean distance differ for embeddings, and which metric is usually preferred for RAG?

expected.heading: Similarity Metrics
expected.text: The choice of similarity metric determines how "closeness" is calculated in vector space: **Cosine similarity** measures the angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude.

cases.referenceAnswer: Cosine similarity compares the angle between vectors and ignores magnitude, while dot product also reflects vector magnitude and equals cosine similarity when vectors are L2-normalized. Euclidean distance measures straight-line distance and is less common for text embeddings because high-dimensional spaces make it less discriminative. For most RAG systems, cosine similarity is the default choice unless the model documentation says otherwise [[Embeddings#Similarity Metrics]].

cases.query: Why can an embedding model work well on benchmarks but still fail on a domain with internal jargon, and what are the suggested mitigations?

expected.heading: Distribution Shift on Internal Terminology
expected.text: A model that scores well on MTEB benchmarks may still fail on your domain.

cases.referenceAnswer: An embedding model can score well on general benchmarks yet still fail in a domain with internal acronyms, product names, or jargon because those terms may be noisy or absent from the model’s training data. As a result, matching queries and documents can end up far apart in vector space even when they use the same terminology. The suggested mitigations are to finetune with domain data or continued pre-training, or to add keyword search in a hybrid retrieval setup so BM25 can handle exact lexical matches [[Embeddings#Distribution Shift on Internal Terminology]].

cases.query: When should I choose fine-tuning instead of prompting or RAG, and what tradeoffs should I expect?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: It is the most powerful and most expensive of the three adaptation levers

expected.heading: When to Fine-tune
expected.text: Reach for fine-tuning only after prompting and retrieval have been tried

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: It matters because knowledge changes faster than model weights

cases.referenceAnswer: Fine-tuning is the most powerful and most expensive adaptation lever, while prompting and RAG are cheaper alternatives that should usually be tried first [[Fine-tuning#Intro]] [[Fine-tuning#When to Fine-tune]] [[RAG#Intro]]. It is best when you need to change model behavior itself, but it bakes that behavior into the weights, so the result persists across requests and can age as the training snapshot becomes stale [[Fine-tuning#Intro]]. RAG is better when the main problem is keeping knowledge current, because it updates knowledge without retraining the model [[RAG#Intro]].

cases.query: What are the main reasons to use fine-tuning for a narrow task, and when is it especially useful?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Fine-tuning continues training a pretrained model's weights on task-specific data, changing the model's behavior rather than just its inputs.

expected.heading: When to Fine-tune
expected.text: The behavior is hard to specify in words but easy to demonstrate

cases.referenceAnswer: Fine-tuning is appropriate when you want to change behavior rather than just provide better instructions or retrieved context [[Fine-tuning#Intro]]. It is especially useful when the desired behavior is hard to describe precisely in words but easy to demonstrate with examples, such as a house style or a nuanced classification boundary [[Fine-tuning#When to Fine-tune]]. It is also a good fit when you want a smaller, cheaper model to match a larger one on a narrow task through distillation [[Fine-tuning#When to Fine-tune]].

cases.query: What are the risks and resource costs of full fine-tuning?

expected.heading: Full Fine-tuning
expected.text: Update every weight in the model.

cases.referenceAnswer: Full fine-tuning updates every weight in the model, which gives it the highest quality ceiling but also makes it expensive to train and serve [[Fine-tuning#Full Fine-tuning]]. Training often needs memory for the model, gradients, and optimizer states, which can be 3–4× the model size in GPU memory, and each fine-tuned task produces a full copy of the model to store and serve [[Fine-tuning#Full Fine-tuning]]. It also risks catastrophic forgetting, where narrow training data erodes the base model’s general capabilities [[Fine-tuning#Full Fine-tuning]].

cases.query: What factors should I use to choose between a small model and a frontier model for a production request, and how do routing patterns help keep cost and latency down?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The engineering job is to match each request to the cheapest model that can handle it

expected.heading: Selection Criteria
expected.text: Choose a model against the actual requirements of the task, not its leaderboard rank

expected.heading: Routing Patterns
expected.text: Send the request to a cheap model first; escalate to a stronger one only when the cheap model fails a check

cases.referenceAnswer: Choose the model based on the task’s actual requirements, not leaderboard rank: difficulty, context length, capabilities, latency SLA, cost ceiling, privacy, and reliability all matter [[Model Selection and Routing#Selection Criteria]]. The goal is to route each request to the cheapest model that can handle it, because frontier models are highest quality but slowest and most expensive, while small models are cheaper and faster but weaker [[Model Selection and Routing#Intro]]. A cascade routing pattern can keep most traffic cheap by trying a small model first and escalating only when the output is low confidence or invalid [[Model Selection and Routing#Routing Patterns]].

cases.query: When should a system use cascade routing versus classifier routing, and what is the main tradeoff between them?

expected.heading: Routing Patterns
expected.text: Send the request to a cheap model first; escalate to a stronger one only when the cheap model fails a check

cases.referenceAnswer: Cascade routing sends each request to a cheap model first and only escalates when the result fails a check such as low confidence or invalid output [[Model Selection and Routing#Routing Patterns]]. Classifier routing instead labels the query up front and dispatches it directly to the right model, rather than waiting for a failure [[Model Selection and Routing#Routing Patterns]]. The main tradeoff is that cascade can be simpler and cheaper for easy traffic, while classifier routing avoids failed first attempts by making the routing decision earlier [[Model Selection and Routing#Routing Patterns]].

cases.query: How do prompt text and generation settings each affect the behavior of an LLM, and which settings are useful for controlling schema-like outputs?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Prompt engineering is the practice of turning a vague user intention into a precise model task.

expected.heading: LLM Settings
expected.text: Prompt text controls intent, while generation settings control sampling behavior and output boundaries.

expected.heading: Prompt Anatomy
expected.text: Most effective prompts combine four elements

cases.referenceAnswer: Prompt text is used to express the task itself, while generation settings shape how the model samples and where it stops [[Prompting#LLM Settings]]. Effective prompts usually include an instruction, context, input data, and an output indicator so the model has less ambiguity about what to do and how to format the result [[Prompting#Prompt Anatomy]]. For schema-like or multi-part outputs, stop sequences are especially useful because they explicitly terminate generation at the right boundary [[Prompting#LLM Settings]].

cases.query: What is the basic flow of a RAG system, and what are the main production patterns that build on it?

expected.heading: Core Flow
expected.text: ```mermaid flowchart LR Q[User Query] --> T[Query Translation] T --> R[Retrieval and Fusion] R --> RR[Optional Reranking] RR --> C[Context Assembly] C --> G[LLM Generation] G --> V[Groundedness and Ci

expected.heading: Choosing a Pattern
expected.text: The short version, in adoption order:

cases.referenceAnswer: A basic RAG system translates the user query, retrieves and fuses evidence, optionally reranks it, assembles context, generates an answer, and then checks groundedness and citations [[RAG#Core Flow]]. In production, the main patterns build from a baseline single-pass retrieve-then-generate setup into more advanced approaches such as hybrid search plus reranking, query rewriting and routing, parent-document retrieval, and multi-query fusion [[RAG#Choosing a Pattern]]. The baseline single-pass pattern is the mandatory starting point and measurement baseline [[RAG#Choosing a Pattern]].

cases.query: How should a team evaluate a RAG system so they can tell whether a quality drop comes from retrieval, generation, or end-to-end behavior?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: RAG evaluation decomposes into three layers: retrieval quality, generation quality, and end-to-end usefulness.

expected.heading: Retrieval Quality Metrics
expected.text: Retrieval quality metrics require a labeled evaluation set

expected.heading: LLM-as-Judge Metrics
expected.text: For semantic quality, run an LLM judge asynchronously on a sampled fraction of production traffic.

expected.heading: Performance and Cost Metrics
expected.text: Set SLOs on p95 end-to-end, but always diagnose regressions with per-stage breakdown.

cases.referenceAnswer: A RAG evaluation plan should separate retrieval quality, generation quality, and end-to-end usefulness so you can localize regressions instead of just seeing that “quality dropped.” Retrieval should be measured with labeled query sets and metrics like Recall@k, generation should be checked with an LLM judge for faithfulness, and system health should also be tracked with end-to-end and per-stage latency metrics. [[RAG Evaluation#Intro]] [[Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics]] [[Monitoring#LLM-as-Judge Metrics]] [[Monitoring#Performance and Cost Metrics]]

cases.query: What is the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations, and which detection approach is best suited to each?

expected.heading: Intrinsic vs Extrinsic
expected.text: Ji et al.

expected.heading: Detection
expected.text: Use multiple detectors because each catches different failure modes.

cases.referenceAnswer: Intrinsic hallucinations contradict facts already present in the supplied context, while extrinsic hallucinations add unsupported facts that are not in the source material. NLI-based fact checking is especially strong for intrinsic cases because it can directly compare claims against the context and detect contradictions. Extrinsic hallucinations are harder to catch with context-only checks and often need external verification; self-consistency can help flag unstable or contradictory outputs, but it does not by itself prove factual correctness. [[Hallucinations#Intrinsic vs Extrinsic]] [[Hallucinations#Detection]]

cases.query: How can a team reduce hallucinations caused by generation settings and output formatting in a production LLM system?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Hallucination is a correctness failure where an LLM output sounds fluent and confident but is not supported by evidence or reality.

expected.heading: Structured Output
expected.text: Structured output ensures the model returns data in a machine-parsable format (JSON, function calls, enums) rather than free text.

expected.heading: Temperature Miscalibration
expected.text: Setting temperature too high for factual tasks introduces token-level randomness that manifests as hallucinated details, inconsistent formatting, and unreliable structured output.

cases.referenceAnswer: A production system can reduce hallucinations by lowering temperature for factual tasks and by using constrained structured outputs when the response must be machine-readable. Higher temperature increases token-level randomness and can inject invented specifics, while constrained decoding forces the model to emit only schema-valid tokens. JSON mode is weaker because it only guarantees valid JSON, not a specific schema, so it is less protective against semantically bad outputs. [[Hallucinations#Intro]] [[Generation#Structured Output]] [[Generation#Temperature Miscalibration]]

cases.query: Why is a golden query set important for retrieval evaluation, and what metric is usually used to judge whether the right documents are being found?

expected.heading: Retrieval Quality Metrics
expected.text: Retrieval quality metrics require a labeled evaluation set

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Retrieval metrics measure whether the right evidence reaches the generator.

cases.referenceAnswer: A golden query set is important because retrieval metrics need labeled queries with known relevant documents, which lets teams evaluate retrieval on a scheduled basis instead of only watching live traffic. The main metric is Recall@k, which measures what fraction of relevant documents appear in the top-k results. This matters because retrieval has to get the right evidence into the prompt before generation can succeed. [[Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics]] [[RAG Evaluation#Intro]]

cases.query: When should I choose a Dictionary instead of a List or HashSet in .NET, and why does that matter for performance?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A data structure organizes data for efficient access, mutation, and iteration.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- What is a data structure? Which ones do you know? Which of them exist in .NET? > A data structure is a way to organize related data into a collection-like object.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` is the primary key-value collection in .NET and the default choice for fast lookups by key in single-threaded or externally synchronized code.

cases.referenceAnswer: Choose a Dictionary when you need fast lookup, insertion, and deletion by a unique key, while a List is better for ordered, index-based access and a HashSet is for membership testing. In .NET, Dictionary<TKey, TValue> is the primary key-value collection and is the default choice for fast lookups by key in single-threaded or externally synchronized code. Picking the right collection often matters more than micro-optimizing the code that uses it. [[Data Structures#Intro]] [[Data Structures#Questions]] [[Dictionary#Intro]]

cases.query: How does a Dictionary<TKey, TValue> work internally, and what can go wrong if keys are mutable or GetHashCode/Equals are inconsistent?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Internally it is a hash table: keys are mapped to buckets by hash code, then equality checks resolve collisions within each bucket.

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: `Dictionary` is hash-table based: keys are mapped to buckets by hash code, then equality checks resolve collisions.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - **`GetHashCode`/`Equals` contract violation** — if `Equals` says two keys are equal but `GetHashCode` returns different values, the dictionary stores both as separate entries.

cases.referenceAnswer: A Dictionary is hash-table based: keys are mapped to buckets by hash code, and equality checks resolve collisions within each bucket. Because of that design, if Equals and GetHashCode disagree, the dictionary can store logically equal keys as separate entries and later lookups may miss one of them. Mutable keys are also dangerous because changing a field used in hashing after insertion can orphan the entry in the wrong bucket. [[Dictionary#Intro]] [[Dictionary#Deeper Explanation]] [[Dictionary#Pitfalls]]

cases.query: When does algorithmic complexity matter less than constant factors, and how should that affect the choice between data structure and algorithm optimization?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: When input sizes are small and bounded (e.g., iterating over 10 HTTP headers), constant factors and cache locality dominate.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: choosing the right collection, avoiding quadratic blowups in batch processing, and reasoning about tradeoffs before writing code.

cases.referenceAnswer: When input sizes are small and bounded, constant factors and cache locality can matter more than asymptotic complexity, so a theoretically better algorithm may still be slower. The guidance here is to start with the data structure, because the right structure can remove the need for a clever algorithm. In production-scale systems, the broader goal is to avoid bad collection choices that create quadratic blowups or other avoidable performance problems. [[02 Computer Science#Questions]] [[02 Computer Science#Intro]]

cases.query: How do prompt injection risks differ between direct, indirect, and multimodal attacks in LLM applications, and why are retrieval pathways especially risky?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.citationLabel: 11 AI & ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md
expected.text: The 2025 refresh keeps Prompt Injection as the top risk and expands it to multimodal vectors.

expected.heading: Prompt Injection (LLM01)
expected.citationLabel: 11 AI & ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md
expected.text: **Mechanism**: The model receives attacker instructions in the same natural-language channel as legitimate instructions, so it may follow malicious text even when system guidance says not to.

cases.referenceAnswer: Prompt injection remains the top LLM risk in the 2025 OWASP list, and the refresh explicitly expands it to multimodal vectors [[OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM#Intro]]. Direct injection happens when malicious instructions are placed directly in the user prompt, while indirect injection plants instructions in retrieved or browsed content [[OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM#Prompt Injection (LLM01)]]. The section also notes that retrieval pathways can become execution pathways when trust boundaries are crossed [[OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM#Prompt Injection (LLM01)]].
expectedCitations: 11 AI & ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md

cases.query: What is the difference between RAG and fine-tuning, and when should each be chosen for a changing product policy?

expected.heading: RAG vs Fine-Tuning
expected.text: RAG externalizes knowledge into retrievable sources, while fine-tuning changes model behavior in weights.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Conversely, fine-tuning cannot fix missing or stale knowledge — it bakes in a snapshot that starts aging immediately and provides no source traceability.

cases.referenceAnswer: RAG externalizes knowledge into retrievable sources, while fine-tuning changes the model’s behavior in its weights [[RAG#RAG vs Fine-Tuning]]. For a product policy that changes weekly, RAG is the better fit because it can be updated by reindexing documents and keeps source traceability high [[RAG#RAG vs Fine-Tuning]]. Fine-tuning is not the right tool for missing or stale knowledge because it bakes in a snapshot that ages immediately and does not provide source traceability [[RAG#Questions]].

cases.query: What guardrail layers should be used around an LLM system, and what does the defense-in-depth flow look like?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Guardrails are layered controls around an LLM that reduce risk: they prevent unsafe actions, limit data exposure, and keep outputs within policy and quality constraints.

expected.heading: Defense-in-Depth Model
expected.text: U[User Input] --> IG[Input Guardrails]

cases.referenceAnswer: Guardrails are layered controls around an LLM that reduce risk by preventing unsafe actions, limiting data exposure, and keeping outputs within policy and quality constraints [[Guardrails#Intro]]. The defense-in-depth model applies controls across input, context, output, and runtime layers rather than relying on a single safety filter [[Guardrails#Intro]]. The flow starts with user input passing through input guardrails before reaching the LLM, then continues through output and runtime guardrails before a response is returned [[Guardrails#Defense-in-Depth Model]].

cases.query: Which input guardrails are recommended to stop prompt injection and other unsafe requests before they reach the model?

expected.heading: Input Guardrails
expected.text: Validate and filter what reaches the LLM:

cases.referenceAnswer: Recommended input guardrails include prompt injection detection, intent classification, content filtering, and input length limits [[Guardrails#Input Guardrails]]. Prompt injection detection looks for attempts to override system instructions, while intent classification can reject out-of-scope requests before they reach the LLM [[Guardrails#Input Guardrails]]. Content filtering and input length limits add further protection against disallowed content and context stuffing attacks [[Guardrails#Input Guardrails]].

cases.query: Why can adding more retrieved documents or a longer prompt make answers worse, and what should you do instead?

expected.heading: Lost-in-the-Middle Attention Failure
expected.text: When relevant evidence lands in the middle of a long context, the model underweights it relative to evidence at the start or end.

expected.heading: More Context, Worse Answers
expected.text: **What goes wrong**: a team adds more retrieved documents or a longer system prompt expecting better answers, and quality drops or latency spikes for no visible reason.

cases.referenceAnswer: Adding more context can hurt because attention dilutes across a larger window and important evidence may land in the low-attention middle, where it is underweighted. The recommended fix is to optimize for signal density rather than volume by reranking and filtering to fewer, higher-quality chunks and ordering them by relevance. For high-stakes cases, place key evidence at both the start and end of the context. [[Generation#Lost-in-the-Middle Attention Failure]] [[Context Engineering#More Context, Worse Answers]]

cases.query: How should a RAG system be monitored so that hallucinations are not mistaken for being solved after rollout?

expected.heading: RAG Does Not Eliminate Hallucinations
expected.text: teams ship RAG and assume hallucination is solved, then stop active monitoring.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combines retrieval and generation: retrieve evidence from your corpus, then generate an answer grounded in that evidence.

expected.heading: Core Flow
expected.text: ```mermaid flowchart LR Q[User Query] --> T[Query Translation] T --> R[Retrieval and Fusion] R --> RR[Optional Reranking] RR --> C[Context Assembly] C --> G[LLM Generation] G --> V[Groundedness and Ci

cases.referenceAnswer: RAG does not eliminate hallucinations, so teams should not stop monitoring after rollout. The system should track retrieval recall and faithfulness separately and keep claim-to-context verification in place, because retrieval miss, context overflow, and model additions beyond retrieved evidence can still occur. RAG is meant to ground generation in retrieved evidence, but it remains a pipeline with multiple failure points. [[Hallucinations#RAG Does Not Eliminate Hallucinations]] [[RAG#Intro]] [[RAG#Core Flow]]

cases.query: How does a heap maintain priority order during enqueue and dequeue, and what are the time complexities of those operations?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A heap is an implicit complete d ary tree that keeps a priority rule between parent and child nodes.

expected.heading: How It Works
expected.text: Heaps are not globally sorted like a list.

cases.referenceAnswer: A heap keeps only local ordering between parents and children, which is enough to keep the best candidate at the top [[Heap#Intro]] [[Heap#How It Works]]. Enqueue adds the new item at the end and bubbles it up, while dequeue removes the root, moves the last item to the root, and pushes it down [[Heap#How It Works]]. Both operations are O(log n), and peeking the root is O(1) [[Heap#How It Works]].

cases.query: When would you choose a heap-based priority queue for graph or scheduling work in .NET, and what property makes it suitable?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A heap is an implicit complete d ary tree that keeps a priority rule between parent and child nodes.

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: The most common representation is an adjacency list: each node maps to its neighbors.

cases.referenceAnswer: A heap-based priority queue is a good fit when you need fast repeated insertion and removal of the next highest-priority item, such as in schedulers and path finding workloads [[Heap#Intro]]. In .NET, `PriorityQueue<TElement, TPriority>` is implemented as an array-backed quaternary heap, which supports those fast enqueue and dequeue operations [[Heap#Intro]]. For graph algorithms, the notes specifically say Dijkstra's algorithm pairs naturally with `PriorityQueue<TElement, int>` in .NET [[Graph#Deeper Explanation]].

cases.query: How do adjacency lists and adjacency matrices compare for sparse graphs, and how are weighted graphs represented in .NET?

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: The most common representation is an adjacency list: each node maps to its neighbors.

cases.referenceAnswer: An adjacency list is the most common representation, where each node maps to its neighbors, and it uses O(V + E) space, making it efficient for sparse graphs [[Graph#Deeper Explanation]]. An adjacency matrix uses O(V²) space and gives O(1) edge-existence checks, but it wastes memory when the graph is sparse [[Graph#Deeper Explanation]]. For weighted graphs, the notes say an adjacency list can be represented as `Dictionary<int, List<(int neighbor, int weight)>>`, while a matrix can store weights in an `int[,]` with a sentinel for absent edges [[Graph#Deeper Explanation]].

cases.query: How does a hash map achieve fast key lookups, and what happens when hash distribution is poor or keys are mutable?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A hash map stores key-value pairs and uses hashing to find a bucket quickly.

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: Hash maps use two rules together: hash distribution and equality checks.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Mutable key fields break lookups

cases.referenceAnswer: A hash map stores key-value pairs and uses hashing to find a bucket quickly, then uses equality checks to resolve collisions. Good hash distribution keeps buckets short and operations fast, but if a key is mutated after insertion, lookups can fail because the entry is left in the wrong bucket. A poor hash function can also collapse performance to O(n) by putting every entry into one bucket [[HashMap#Intro]] [[HashMap#Deeper Explanation]] [[HashMap#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: When should I use Dictionary<TKey, TValue> in .NET, and what are the main pitfalls to avoid?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` is the primary key-value collection in .NET and the default choice for fast lookups by key in single-threaded or externally synchronized code.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - **`GetHashCode`/`Equals` contract violation** — if `Equals` says two keys are equal but `GetHashCode` returns different values, the dictionary stores both as separate entries.

cases.referenceAnswer: Dictionary<TKey, TValue> is the primary key-value collection in .NET and the default choice for fast lookups by key in single-threaded or externally synchronized code. It uses hash codes to map keys to buckets and equality checks to resolve collisions, with average O(1) operations and worst-case O(n) behavior. The main pitfalls are violating the GetHashCode/Equals contract, mutating keys after insertion, and performing concurrent writes without synchronization [[Dictionary#Intro]] [[Dictionary#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: What data structure should I use for graph traversal to avoid infinite loops, and when is an adjacency list preferable to an adjacency matrix?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: A DFS or BFS that does not track visited nodes will loop forever on cyclic graphs.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Adjacency list is better for sparse graphs.

cases.referenceAnswer: For graph traversal, you should track visited nodes to avoid infinite loops on cyclic graphs, typically with a HashSet<TNode>. An adjacency list is better for sparse graphs, while an adjacency matrix can be useful for dense graphs with fixed node sets. For weighted shortest-path algorithms, PriorityQueue<TElement, TPriority> is useful [[Graph#Pitfalls]] [[Graph#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: How do hash maps compare with hash sets for lookup-heavy workloads, and what are example use cases for each?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A hash map stores key-value pairs and uses hashing to find a bucket quickly.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `HashSet<T>` stores unique values with O(1) average membership checks.

cases.referenceAnswer: Hash maps are for key-value pairs, while HashSet<T> is for unique values and fast membership checks. Both provide O(1) average lookup-style performance, but a hash set is the better fit when you only need to test whether something exists and do not need to store an associated value. The notes give examples of a session cache using a hash map and a message pipeline deduplicating event IDs with a hash set [[HashMap#Intro]] [[HashSet#Intro]].

cases.query: How does `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` compare with `List` and `ConcurrentDictionary` for lookups and concurrent writes?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: It computes a hash and jumps to a bucket instead of scanning each element.

expected.heading: Hash-Based Collections Comparison
expected.text: Switch to `ConcurrentDictionary` for concurrent writes

expected.heading: Hash-Based Collections Comparison
expected.text: use `Dictionary<TKey,TValue>` by default.

cases.referenceAnswer: `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` is usually faster than a `List` for lookups because it computes a hash and jumps to a bucket instead of scanning each element [[Dictionary#Questions]]. For general use, the default choice is `Dictionary<TKey,TValue>` [[HashMap#Hash-Based Collections Comparison]]. If you need concurrent read/write access without external locks, switch to `ConcurrentDictionary<TKey,TValue>` [[Dictionary#Hash-Based Collections Comparison]].

cases.query: Why can hash map performance degrade from O(1) to O(n), and what does that imply about hash quality?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Excessive collisions put many keys in the same bucket, so operations must compare more entries.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: More collisions increase comparisons in a bucket chain and can push operations toward O(n).

cases.referenceAnswer: Hash map performance can degrade from O(1) to O(n) when excessive collisions put many keys in the same bucket, forcing more comparisons [[HashMap#Questions]]. A bad `GetHashCode` implementation is also a correctness risk because equal keys must produce equal hashes for lookups to work reliably [[HashMap#Questions]]. In short, good hash distribution matters for both speed and correctness [[Dictionary#Questions]].

cases.query: When should you use an adjacency list versus an adjacency matrix for a graph in .NET, and what are the tradeoffs?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: In .NET, there is no built-in `Graph<T>` type

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: The most common representation is an adjacency list: each node maps to its neighbors.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Always match representation to graph density.

cases.referenceAnswer: In .NET, there is no built-in `Graph<T>` type, so graphs are composed from other collections such as adjacency lists or matrices [[Graph#Intro]]. The most common representation is an adjacency list, which is efficient for sparse graphs, while an adjacency matrix uses O(V²) space but gives O(1) edge-existence checks [[Graph#Deeper Explanation]]. The choice should match graph density, because a matrix can waste substantial memory on sparse graphs [[Graph#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: What are the main pitfalls of graph traversal and how can they be avoided?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Always maintain a `HashSet<TNode>` of visited nodes.

cases.referenceAnswer: A DFS or BFS that does not track visited nodes can loop forever on cyclic graphs, so you should maintain a `HashSet<TNode>` of visited nodes [[Graph#Pitfalls]]. Recursive DFS can also overflow the stack on deep graphs, so iterative DFS with an explicit `Stack<TNode>` is safer for unknown-depth cases [[Graph#Pitfalls]]. Finally, choose the graph representation based on density so you do not waste memory on sparse graphs [[Graph#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: Why can a HashSet contain duplicates or fail to find an item when Equals is overridden incorrectly?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - **Broken `GetHashCode`/`Equals` contract** — overriding `Equals` without matching `GetHashCode` causes `Add` to accept duplicates and `Contains` to miss existing entries.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- What is the difference between `HashSet<T>` and `List<T>` for membership checks? > `HashSet<T>.Contains` is O(1) average; `List<T>.Contains` is O(n).

cases.referenceAnswer: A `HashSet<T>` can behave incorrectly if `Equals` and `GetHashCode` are not consistent: two objects that are equal must produce the same hash code, or `Add` may accept duplicates and `Contains` may miss an existing entry [[HashSet#Pitfalls]]. This is the same reason `HashSet<T>.Contains` can fail for logically equal objects when the hash/equality contract is broken [[HashSet#Questions]].

cases.query: What happens if you mutate a field used in a HashSet's hash code after inserting the item?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - **Broken `GetHashCode`/`Equals` contract** — overriding `Equals` without matching `GetHashCode` causes `Add` to accept duplicates and `Contains` to miss existing entries.

cases.referenceAnswer: If you change a field that participates in `GetHashCode` after an item has been added, the set may no longer be able to find that item because it becomes unreachable in its bucket [[HashSet#Pitfalls]]. In that case, `Contains` can return `false` even though the entry is still present [[HashSet#Pitfalls]]. The safe approach is to use immutable types or avoid mutating hash-participating fields after insertion [[HashSet#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: When should I use HashSet instead of Dictionary or SortedSet?

expected.heading: Hash-Based Collections Comparison
expected.text: | Type | Stores | Thread-safe | When to use | |---|---|---|---| | `HashSet<T>` | Values only | No | Unique membership checks, set operations | | `Dictionary<TKey,TValue>` | Key-value pairs | No | Key-

cases.referenceAnswer: Use `HashSet<T>` when you only need membership tracking or set operations such as union, intersect, and except [[HashSet#Hash-Based Collections Comparison]]. Use `Dictionary<TKey,TValue>` when you need to associate a value with each key [[HashSet#Hash-Based Collections Comparison]]. Use `SortedSet<T>` when you want sorted uniqueness and are willing to pay `O(log n)` operations [[HashSet#Hash-Based Collections Comparison]].

cases.query: Why is HashSet enumeration order unstable, and what should I use if order matters?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - **Broken `GetHashCode`/`Equals` contract** — overriding `Equals` without matching `GetHashCode` causes `Add` to accept duplicates and `Contains` to miss existing entries.

cases.referenceAnswer: `HashSet<T>` does not guarantee a stable enumeration order because the order depends on internal bucket layout and can change after `Add` or `Remove` operations, or even across .NET versions [[HashSet#Pitfalls]]. If you need deterministic ordering, use `SortedSet<T>` or sort the results with `.OrderBy()` [[HashSet#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: Why can a hash map look like it has O(1) lookups but still degrade to O(n), and what role do collisions and GetHashCode play?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - **Mutable key fields break lookups** — if you insert a key, then mutate a field that participates in `GetHashCode`, the entry becomes orphaned in the wrong bucket.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Excessive collisions put many keys in the same bucket, so operations must compare more entries.

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: `HashSet<T>` is hash-based: `GetHashCode` determines the bucket; `Equals` resolves collisions within a bucket.

cases.referenceAnswer: A hash map can degrade from O(1) to O(n) when many keys collide into the same bucket, forcing more comparisons per operation. A bad GetHashCode implementation can cause those collisions, and hash maps depend on the hash and equality contracts to work correctly. [[HashMap#Pitfalls]] [[HashMap#Questions]] [[HashSet#Deeper Explanation]]

cases.query: When should I choose HashSet<T> instead of List<T> or a sorted map, and what tradeoff does that imply?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Hash map vs sorted map: hash map favors fast point lookups, sorted map favors ordered iteration.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Use it when uniqueness and lookup speed matter more than ordering.

cases.referenceAnswer: Choose HashSet<T> when you need uniqueness and fast membership checks, especially for repeated lookups by key. Compared with a sorted map, a hash-based structure favors point lookups over ordered iteration, and compared with a list scan it is better for repeated lookups but may be unnecessary for very small fixed data sets. [[HashMap#Tradeoffs]] [[HashSet#Intro]]

cases.query: How do HashSet<T> set operations work, and which ones mutate the set versus just test relationships?

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: `HashSet<T>` is hash-based: `GetHashCode` determines the bucket; `Equals` resolves collisions within a bucket.

cases.referenceAnswer: HashSet<T> supports set algebra operations such as UnionWith, IntersectWith, ExceptWith, and SymmetricExceptWith, and these are in-place mutations. The relationship checks IsSubsetOf, IsSupersetOf, and SetEquals do not modify the set. [[HashSet#Deeper Explanation]]

cases.query: What are the common pitfalls of using a hash map with custom keys in .NET, and what type is the standard hash map?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Mutable key fields break lookups

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- Why can hash map performance degrade from O(1) to O(n)? > Excessive collisions put many keys in the same bucket, so operations must compare more entries.

cases.referenceAnswer: With custom keys, mutating any field that participates in GetHashCode can orphan the entry and make lookups fail. The safe approach is to use immutable key types or avoid changing key fields after insertion. In modern .NET, the standard hash map is Dictionary<TKey, TValue>. [[HashMap#Pitfalls]] [[HashMap#Questions]]

cases.query: When should I choose LinkedList<T> over List<T> for editing and traversal workloads?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: It is useful when you already keep node references and need frequent O(1) inserts/removes around those nodes.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - Using `LinkedList<T>` for index-based access causes O(n) scans and can underperform `List<T>` significantly.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: linked list wins for O(1) local edits around known nodes; list usually wins for traversal and random access.

cases.referenceAnswer: Choose LinkedList<T> when you already have node references and need frequent O(1) inserts or removes around those nodes. For index-heavy, traversal-heavy, or random-access workloads, List<T> is usually the better choice because linked lists scan by index and have worse locality. In short, LinkedList<T> is best for local edits around known nodes, while List<T> is better for traversal and random access [[LinkedList#Intro]] [[LinkedList#Pitfalls]] [[LinkedList#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: What happens if I use a LinkedListNode<T> from another list with AddBefore, AddAfter, or Remove?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - Using `LinkedList<T>` for index-based access causes O(n) scans and can underperform `List<T>` significantly.

cases.referenceAnswer: A detached or foreign LinkedListNode<T> cannot be used as an anchor for AddBefore, AddAfter, or Remove, because the node must belong to the target list context. The guidance is to check node.List before using a node handle and to re-find or re-add nodes when needed [[LinkedList#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: Why can LinkedList<T> be slower than List<T> even when the algorithmic complexity looks similar?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Random index access is O(n) because traversal is required.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Pointer-rich node allocation hurts cache locality, so iteration can be slower even when complexity looks similar on paper.

cases.referenceAnswer: LinkedList<T> can be slower because random index access requires traversal, making it O(n), and each element carries pointer overhead that hurts memory locality. The notes also say pointer-rich allocation can slow iteration even when the big-O complexity seems similar. That is why linked lists can underperform List<T> in index-heavy or traversal-heavy workloads [[LinkedList#Intro]] [[LinkedList#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: When is LinkedList<T> preferable to Queue<T> or Stack<T>?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: - `LinkedList<T>` vs `List<T>`: linked list wins for O(1) local edits around known nodes; list usually wins for traversal and random access.

cases.referenceAnswer: LinkedList<T> is preferable when you need O(1) local edits around known nodes, not just FIFO or LIFO behavior. If you only need queue or stack semantics, Queue<T> or Stack<T> are simpler and often faster. So LinkedList<T> is the better fit for node-local editing, while Queue<T> and Stack<T> are better for pure FIFO/LIFO use cases [[LinkedList#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: How does Queue<T> work internally in .NET, and why are Enqueue and Dequeue O(1) on average?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Queue<T>` is a FIFO (first in, first out) collection.

cases.referenceAnswer: Queue<T> is a FIFO collection, so the earliest enqueued item is processed first. In .NET it is implemented as a circular buffer, where Enqueue adds at the tail and Dequeue removes from the head, with head and tail indices wrapping around instead of shifting elements [[Queue#Intro]]. Because the indices advance modulo the array length, the operations are O(1) on average, with only occasional O(n) resize copies when the buffer fills [[Queue#Intro]].

cases.query: When should I use Span<T> instead of T[] or Memory<T>, and what are the main tradeoffs?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Span<T>` is a stack-only view over contiguous memory.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: spans avoid copies for slicing, arrays are simpler when you need ownership and long-lived storage.

cases.referenceAnswer: Use Span<T> when you need a stack-only view over contiguous memory for high-performance slicing or parsing with minimal allocations. It is a good fit for synchronous fast paths because it avoids copies for slicing, while arrays are simpler when you need ownership and long-lived storage. If the data must survive async boundaries, Memory<T> is the better choice. [[Span#Intro]] [[Span#Tradeoffs]]

cases.query: Why can't a Span<T> be returned from a method if it points to stack memory, and what should be used instead for async code?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - Returning a `Span<T>` that points to stack memory is invalid because the underlying buffer is gone after method return.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Span<T>` is a stack-only view over contiguous memory.

cases.referenceAnswer: A Span<T> that points to stack memory cannot be returned because the stack buffer no longer exists after the method returns. More generally, Span<T> cannot cross await boundaries because it is stack-only and cannot escape to the managed heap. For async flows, use Memory<T> instead. [[Span#Pitfalls]] [[Span#Intro]]

cases.query: What are the main characteristics of Span<T> as a memory view, and what kinds of memory can it wrap?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Span<T>` is a stack-only view over contiguous memory.

cases.referenceAnswer: Span<T> is a stack-only view over contiguous memory that does not own the data. It can point to existing memory such as array segments, stack-allocated buffers, or unmanaged memory. Slicing a span creates lightweight subviews without copying data. [[Span#Intro]]

cases.query: When should I use Stack<T> instead of recursion for depth-first search, and what tradeoff does it avoid?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Use it for backtracking, undo flows, and depth-first traversals.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: - `Stack<T>` vs `Queue<T>`: stack favors LIFO workflows, queue favors FIFO processing pipelines.

cases.referenceAnswer: Use Stack<T> for depth-first traversals or backtracking when you want an explicit LIFO structure instead of recursion. The main tradeoff is that recursion is concise, but an explicit Stack<T> avoids deep-recursion stack-overflow risk. Stack<T> is also described as mirroring the runtime call stack’s LIFO discipline, which makes it a natural fit for these workflows [[Stack#Intro]] [[Stack#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: What happens if I call Pop or Peek on an empty Stack<T>, and what should I check first?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - Calling `Pop`/`Peek` on an empty stack throws `InvalidOperationException`.

cases.referenceAnswer: Calling Pop or Peek on an empty Stack<T> throws InvalidOperationException. If emptiness is possible, you should check Count first before calling either method [[Stack#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: How does Stack<T> differ from Queue<T> in processing order, and what bug can happen if I choose the wrong one?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Stack<T>` is a LIFO (last in, first out) collection.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: stack favors LIFO workflows, queue favors FIFO processing pipelines.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - Calling `Pop`/`Peek` on an empty stack throws `InvalidOperationException`.

cases.referenceAnswer: Stack<T> is LIFO, so the most recently pushed item is processed first, while Queue<T> is FIFO and is better for processing pipelines. If you use Stack<T> for queue-like workflows, it reverses the processing order and can cause subtle logic bugs [[Stack#Intro]] [[Stack#Tradeoffs]] [[Stack#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: How does enumeration order work for Stack<T>, and how does that relate to its top-of-stack behavior?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Enumeration is from top to bottom.

cases.referenceAnswer: Stack<T> enumerates from top to bottom, which matches its LIFO behavior. In other words, the most recently pushed element is the first one popped [[Stack#Intro]].

cases.query: How do balanced trees compare with unbalanced trees in terms of height and operation complexity, and what happens when you insert already-sorted values into a naive BST?

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: Balanced trees keep height at O(log n), making insert, delete, and search O(log n).

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: inserting already-sorted values into a naive BST produces a linked list with O(n) operations.

cases.referenceAnswer: Balanced trees keep height at O(log n), so insert, delete, and search are O(log n), while unbalanced trees can degrade toward O(n). Inserting already-sorted values into a naive BST can turn it into a linked list, which causes O(n) behavior. [[Trees#Deeper Explanation]] [[Trees#Pitfalls]]

cases.query: What traversal order should be used when you need to process children before parents, and how does that relate to evaluating an expression tree or deleting a subtree?

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: A tree organizes nodes so each node has at most one parent (except the root) and any number of children.

cases.referenceAnswer: Post-order traversal is the right choice when children must be processed before parents. The section gives deleting a subtree and evaluating an expression tree as examples of this use case. [[Trees#Deeper Explanation]]

cases.query: Why might a trie be preferred over scanning a Dictionary for autocomplete, and what lookup complexity does it provide?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: an autocomplete service maintains a trie (prefix tree) of 500K product names; lookup for any prefix completes in O(k) where k is prefix length, regardless of dataset size

cases.referenceAnswer: A trie is preferred for autocomplete because prefix lookup depends on the prefix length rather than the total number of stored items. In the example, lookup completes in O(k), where k is the prefix length, while a Dictionary scan would be O(n). [[Trees#Intro]]

cases.query: What are the main pitfalls of recursive traversal on very deep trees, and what alternative is suggested for unknown-depth trees?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: a tree with 100K+ nodes and no balancing guarantee (e.g., user-built BST from sorted input) can degrade to a linked list with depth 100K.

cases.referenceAnswer: Very deep, unbalanced trees can degrade into linked-list-like structures with extreme depth, which makes recursive DFS risky because it can blow the default 1 MB stack. For trees with unknown depth, the section recommends iterative traversal with an explicit Stack<T>. [[Trees#Pitfalls]]

cases.query: Why is Dictionary<TKey, TValue> preferred over Hashtable in new .NET code, and what collision strategy does Hashtable use internally?

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: `Hashtable` stores keys and values as `object`, so value types are boxed/unboxed.

expected.heading: Hash-Based Collections Comparison
expected.text: | Type | Key type | Type-safe | When to use | |---|---|---|---| | `Hashtable` | `object` | No | Legacy interop only | | `Dictionary<TKey,TValue>` | Generic | Yes | Default key-value map in modern .NET

cases.referenceAnswer: Hashtable is less preferred because it stores keys and values as object, which means value types are boxed and unboxed, and because it is a legacy choice rather than the default modern map. Internally, it uses separate chaining, so collisions create a linked list in the bucket and lookups can degrade to O(n) in the worst case. The note recommends migrating to Dictionary<TKey,TValue> for type safety and better performance [[Hashtable#Deeper Explanation]] [[Hashtable#Hash-Based Collections Comparison]].

cases.query: How does collision handling in Hashtable affect lookup performance, and how does that compare to Dictionary<TKey, TValue>?

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: Lookup traverses the chain until the matching key is found, making worst-case performance O(n) when all keys collide.

cases.referenceAnswer: Hashtable resolves collisions with separate chaining, so a lookup may need to traverse a linked list in the bucket until it finds the key. If all keys collide, the worst-case lookup time becomes O(n). Dictionary<TKey,TValue> instead uses open addressing with prime-based probing, which is described as more cache-friendly and avoids per-collision heap allocations [[Hashtable#Deeper Explanation]].

cases.query: What is the decision rule for choosing between Hashtable and Dictionary<TKey,TValue> in modern .NET?

expected.heading: Hash-Based Collections Comparison
expected.text: Default key-value map in modern .NET

cases.referenceAnswer: The decision rule is to avoid Hashtable in new code and use Dictionary<TKey,TValue> instead. Dictionary<TKey,TValue> is the default key-value map in modern .NET, while Hashtable is listed as legacy interop only. The migration recommendation is specifically for type safety and better performance [[Hashtable#Hash-Based Collections Comparison]].

cases.query: How does List<T> grow internally, and why is pre-sizing with a known capacity recommended for performance?

expected.heading: Deeper Explanation
expected.text: `List<T>` stores items in a contiguous array, so indexing by position is O(1) and iterating is cache friendly.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- How is `List<T>` implemented under the hood? > `List<T>` wraps an internal `T[]` buffer and tracks `Count` separately from `Capacity`.

cases.referenceAnswer: `List<T>` stores its elements in an internal array and keeps `Count` separate from `Capacity`, so when the array fills up it allocates a larger one and copies the elements over [[List#Questions]]. The growth strategy doubles capacity, which makes a sequence of appends cost O(n) total and gives amortized O(1) append time [[List#Deeper Explanation]]. If the final size is known ahead of time, constructing with `new List<T>(capacity)` avoids resizes and can reduce allocation and GC pressure [[List#Deeper Explanation]].

cases.query: When should I prefer List<T> over LinkedList<T>, and when is LinkedList<T> the better choice?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: - Prefer `List<T>` over `LinkedList<T>` for most workloads because iteration is faster in practice.

cases.referenceAnswer: `List<T>` is the better default choice for most workloads because iteration is faster in practice [[List#Tradeoffs]]. `LinkedList<T>` is mainly useful when you already have node references and need O(1) inserts or removals around those nodes [[List#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: What is the difference between Count and Capacity in List<T>, and do Clear() or Remove() shrink the underlying storage?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- How is `List<T>` implemented under the hood? > `List<T>` wraps an internal `T[]` buffer and tracks `Count` separately from `Capacity`.

cases.referenceAnswer: `Count` is the number of logical elements, while `Capacity` is the size of the internal array [[List#Questions]]. `Clear()` and `Remove()` usually change only `Count`, not the underlying storage [[List#Questions]]. If you want to shrink memory, use `TrimExcess()` or set `Capacity` [[List#Questions]].

cases.query: How do behavioral patterns differ from the other GoF categories, and when should I choose one?

expected.heading: Behavioral Patterns
expected.text: Behavioral patterns are concerned with algorithms and the assignment of responsibilities between objects

expected.heading: GoF Design Patterns
expected.text: GoF (Gang of Four) Design Patterns are 23 reusable solutions to recurring object-oriented design problems

expected.heading: The Three Categories
expected.text: **[[Software Engineering/05 Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Creational|Creational Patterns]]** (5 patterns) abstract the instantiation process, making systems independent of how objec

cases.referenceAnswer: Behavioral patterns focus on algorithms, responsibilities, and communication between objects, while the GoF catalog as a whole is a set of 23 reusable solutions to recurring object-oriented design problems [[Behavioral#Behavioral Patterns]] [[Design Patterns#GoF Design Patterns]]. The three categories differ in emphasis: creational patterns handle object creation, structural patterns handle composition, and behavioral patterns handle collaboration and decoupling [[Design Patterns#The Three Categories]]. You should reach for behavioral patterns when you need to decouple senders from receivers, make algorithms swappable at runtime, or orchestrate complex workflows [[Behavioral#Behavioral Patterns]] [[Design Patterns#The Three Categories]].

cases.query: How does Chain of Responsibility solve the OrderValidator problem, and what changes in the design?

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `OrderValidator` has one massive `Validate()` method checking stock, fraud, credit, and address — all in sequence with nested if/else: ```csharp public class OrderValidator { private readonly IInvento

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Each validation becomes a handler in a chain.

expected.heading: Chain of Responsibility
expected.text: The Chain of Responsibility pattern passes a request along a chain of handlers

cases.referenceAnswer: The problem is that `OrderValidator` concentrates stock, fraud, credit, and address checks in one large method, so each new rule adds more branching and responsibility to the same class [[Chain of Responsibility#Problem]]. With Chain of Responsibility, each validation becomes its own handler, and the request is passed along the chain until one handler fails or the chain completes [[Chain of Responsibility#Solution]] [[Chain of Responsibility]]. This makes the checks composable and independently testable, and adding a new check does not require changing the existing handlers [[Chain of Responsibility#Solution]] [[Chain of Responsibility]].

cases.query: How does the Command pattern change order operations compared with a direct OrderService, and what capabilities does it add?

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `OrderService` has `PlaceOrder`, `CancelOrder`, `RefundOrder` methods — no undo, no queuing, no audit trail: ```csharp public class OrderService { // ⚠️ No undo — once placed, can't roll back without

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Each operation becomes a command object with `ExecuteAsync()` and `UndoAsync()`: ```csharp // Command interface public interface IOrderCommand { Task ExecuteAsync(); Task UndoAsync(); string Descripti

expected.heading: Command
expected.text: The Command pattern encapsulates a request as an object

cases.referenceAnswer: In the direct `OrderService` design, order actions are separate methods, which leaves no built-in undo, queuing, or audit trail [[Command#Problem]]. With Command, each operation becomes an object that encapsulates the request, its parameters, and the receiver, and it exposes `ExecuteAsync()` and `UndoAsync()` [[Command#Solution]] [[Command#Command]]. That object-based design makes commands serializable, queueable, loggable, retryable, and replayable [[Command#Command]].

cases.query: What is the difference between retrieval metrics and generation metrics in RAG evaluation, and which metric should be tracked as the primary retrieval metric?

expected.heading: Retrieval Metrics
expected.text: Retrieval metrics evaluate whether the relevant documents reached the generator.

expected.heading: Generation Metrics
expected.text: Generation metrics evaluate the quality of the model's output given the retrieved context.

cases.referenceAnswer: Retrieval metrics check whether the relevant documents reached the generator, while generation metrics evaluate the quality of the model’s output given the retrieved context [[RAG Evaluation#Retrieval Metrics]] [[RAG Evaluation#Generation Metrics]]. For retrieval, Recall@k is the primary metric and should always be tracked [[RAG Evaluation#Retrieval Metrics]].

cases.query: How does RAGAS distinguish Context Precision from Faithfulness, and which one needs reference documents?

expected.heading: RAGAS Framework
expected.text: [RAGAS](https://docs.ragas.io/) (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) implements the retrieval and generation concepts above as four named, runnable scores.

cases.referenceAnswer: In RAGAS, Faithfulness is a generation metric that asks whether all claims in the response are supported by retrieved context, while Context Precision is a retrieval metric that asks whether relevant chunks are ranked higher than irrelevant ones [[RAG Evaluation#RAGAS Framework]]. Context Precision needs reference documents, whereas Faithfulness does not [[RAG Evaluation#RAGAS Framework]].

cases.query: Why should RAG monitoring use segmentation and relative alert thresholds instead of only global averages and absolute thresholds?

expected.heading: Segmentation
expected.text: Global aggregate metrics hide localized regressions.

expected.heading: Alerting
expected.text: Effective RAG alerting uses relative thresholds anchored to a rolling baseline, not absolute values.

cases.referenceAnswer: Segmentation is needed because global aggregate metrics can hide localized regressions, such as a change that improves the average while hurting a specific tenant or query cluster [[Monitoring#Segmentation]]. Alerting should use relative thresholds anchored to a rolling baseline because absolute thresholds are brittle across corpus changes, model updates, and seasonal query shifts [[Monitoring#Alerting]]. Together, these practices help catch regressions that averages and fixed cutoffs would miss [[Monitoring#Segmentation]] [[Monitoring#Alerting]].

cases.query: What does LLM-as-a-judge do, and when would you choose absolute scoring versus pairwise comparisons?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: LLM-as-a-judge is an evaluation pattern where one model grades another model's output against an explicit rubric.

expected.heading: Pairwise Comparisons
expected.text: Pairwise comparisons evaluate two candidate outputs side-by-side and pick the better one.

cases.referenceAnswer: LLM-as-a-judge is a pattern where one model grades another model’s output against an explicit rubric, making it useful for scalable semantic evaluation [[LLM-as-a-Judge#Intro]]. Absolute scoring is best when you need hard pass/fail thresholds, while pairwise comparisons are better when quality is subjective or you care more about which answer is better than about a fixed number [[LLM-as-a-Judge#Intro]] [[LLM-as-a-Judge#Pairwise Comparisons]].

cases.query: When should I use hybrid search plus reranking instead of a baseline single-pass RAG setup, and what failure mode does it address?

expected.heading: 1. Baseline Single-Pass RAG
expected.text: The system embeds the user query, retrieves the most similar chunks, places those chunks into the prompt, and asks the model to answer from that context.

expected.heading: 2. Hybrid Search plus Reranking
expected.text: Run lexical search and vector search together, merge their candidates, then rerank the merged set so the generator sees the best few passages.

expected.heading: Diagnostic Combinations
expected.text: Low | High | Noise — retrieval finds relevant docs but drowns them in irrelevant chunks | Re-ranking, tighter metadata filters, reduce k

cases.referenceAnswer: Start with baseline single-pass RAG as the simplest useful loop, but move to hybrid search plus reranking when a single dense top-k search is missing exact terms or returning too much irrelevant context [[RAG Patterns#1. Baseline Single-Pass RAG]] [[RAG Patterns#2. Hybrid Search plus Reranking]]. The diagnostic signal for that upgrade is low context precision with high context recall, which indicates noise rather than missing evidence [[RAG Evaluation#Diagnostic Combinations]].

cases.query: How can I tell whether a retrieval failure is caused by missing evidence versus noisy evidence, and what fixes correspond to each case?

expected.heading: Diagnostic Combinations
expected.text: High | Low | Retrieval ceiling — model uses what it gets correctly, but evidence is missing | Hybrid retrieval, expand k, fix metadata filters, improve embeddings

expected.heading: Component-Level Evaluation
expected.text: A drop in Recall@5 could come from bad chunking (evidence split across boundaries), weak embeddings (model misrepresents domain vocabulary), or poor ANN approximation (index too lossy).

cases.referenceAnswer: If faithfulness is high but context recall is low, the system has a retrieval ceiling: the model is using what it gets correctly, but the needed evidence is missing, so fixes include hybrid retrieval, expanding k, improving metadata filters, or better embeddings [[RAG Evaluation#Diagnostic Combinations]]. If context recall is high but context precision is low, the issue is noise: relevant documents are being drowned in irrelevant chunks, so reranking, tighter filters, or reducing k are the right fixes [[RAG Evaluation#Diagnostic Combinations]]. If the retrieval metric drops, component-level evaluation helps isolate whether the cause is chunking, embeddings, or ANN approximation [[RAG Evaluation#Component-Level Evaluation]].

cases.query: Why does chunking matter for RAG quality, and how does it interact with retrieval evaluation when evidence is split across boundaries?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: If chunks are too wide, retrieval returns noisy context that dilutes the answer; if chunks are too narrow, critical constraints get split across fragments and the model answers from incomplete evidenc

expected.heading: Component-Level Evaluation
expected.text: A drop in Recall@5 could come from bad chunking (evidence split across boundaries), weak embeddings (model misrepresents domain vocabulary), or poor ANN approximation (index too lossy).

expected.heading: 1. Baseline Single-Pass RAG
expected.text: The system embeds the user query, retrieves the most similar chunks, places those chunks into the prompt, and asks the model to answer from that context.
