cases.referenceAnswer: Chunking matters because it defines the retrieval unit: overly wide chunks add noise, while overly narrow chunks can split critical constraints across fragments and leave the model with incomplete evidence [[Chunking#Intro]]. That is why a drop in Recall@5 may reflect bad chunking rather than a retrieval model problem, and component-level evaluation is needed to isolate the bottleneck [[RAG Evaluation#Component-Level Evaluation]]. Baseline single-pass RAG works best when chunking is clean and the answer usually lives in one document [[RAG Patterns#1. Baseline Single-Pass RAG]].

cases.query: When should I choose RAG instead of fine-tuning for a system whose facts change often and must be cited?

expected.heading: RAG vs Fine-Tuning
expected.text: RAG externalizes knowledge into retrievable sources, while fine-tuning changes model behavior in weights.

cases.referenceAnswer: Choose RAG when the system needs fresh facts or source traceability, because RAG keeps knowledge in retrievable sources rather than baking it into weights [[RAG vs Fine-Tuning]]. If product policy changes weekly, RAG can update by reindexing documents, while fine-tuning would require repeated retraining cycles and still provide weak source traceability [[RAG vs Fine-Tuning]]. The decision rule is to start with RAG when facts change often or citation is required [[RAG vs Fine-Tuning]].

cases.query: How does an event bus differ from the Observer pattern, and what problem does it solve in a .NET order-status example?

expected.heading: Event Bus
expected.text: An event bus is a centralized publish/subscribe dispatcher

expected.heading: Observer
expected.text: The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: adding a new subscriber means editing the service

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: event IS the Observer pattern

expected.heading: MediatR Notification Bus
expected.text: the container resolves all handlers at publish time

cases.referenceAnswer: An event bus is a centralized publish/subscribe dispatcher that lets publishers emit events without knowing who listens, while subscribers register handlers without knowing who publishes [[Event Bus#Event Bus]]. By contrast, the Observer pattern ties subscribers to a specific subject instance and notifies dependents when that subject changes [[Observer#Observer]]. In the order-status example, the problem is tight coupling because adding a new subscriber requires editing the service, whereas the event-based solution lets the order raise an event and subscribers register independently [[Observer#Problem]] [[Observer#Solution]].

cases.query: When should you use MediatR as a mediator versus as an event bus, and what is the difference in how each routes work?

expected.heading: Mediator
expected.text: MediatR is the canonical implementation

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: `CheckoutCommand` is sent to the mediator; the handler coordinates the services: ```csharp // Command — data only, no behavior public record CheckoutCommand( Guid CustomerId, IReadOnlyList<OrderItem>

expected.heading: MediatR Notification Bus
expected.text: MediatR's `IPublisher.Publish()` is the simplest in-process event bus in .NET.

expected.heading: Custom Event Bus
expected.text: When you need control over execution strategy

cases.referenceAnswer: MediatR is presented as the canonical implementation of the Mediator pattern, where a request like a checkout command is sent to a mediator and a handler coordinates the work [[Mediator#Mediator]] [[Mediator#Solution]]. The same library can also be used as an in-process event bus through `IPublisher.Publish()`, where an event is published and all registered handlers are resolved at publish time [[Event Bus#MediatR Notification Bus]]. If you need explicit control over execution strategy such as parallelism, ordering, or error isolation, the notes recommend a custom event bus instead of relying on MediatR's default configuration [[Event Bus#Custom Event Bus]].

cases.query: How do the mediator and event bus patterns differ in a checkout/order workflow, and what coupling problem does each one address?

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `CheckoutController` directly calls 4 services — all coupled through the controller, which becomes a god class: ```csharp [ApiController] public class CheckoutController( IInventoryService inventory,

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: `CheckoutCommand` is sent to the mediator; the handler coordinates the services: ```csharp // Command — data only, no behavior public record CheckoutCommand( Guid CustomerId, IReadOnlyList<OrderItem>

expected.heading: Event Bus
expected.text: An event bus is a centralized publish/subscribe dispatcher: publishers emit events into the bus without knowing who listens, and subscribers register handlers by event type without knowing who publish

expected.heading: Mediator
expected.text: Instead of components referring to each other directly

expected.heading: MediatR Notification Bus
expected.text: MediatR's `IPublisher.Publish()` is the simplest in-process event bus in .NET.

cases.referenceAnswer: The checkout example shows a mediator replacing a controller that directly coordinates multiple services, moving the orchestration into a handler so callers only send a command [[Mediator#Problem]] [[Mediator#Solution]]. The mediator pattern reduces direct component-to-component coupling by routing interaction through a central object, while the event bus goes further by decoupling publishers and subscribers so neither side knows the other exists [[Mediator#Mediator]] [[Event Bus#Event Bus]]. In the event-bus example, handlers are registered for an event and resolved at publish time, which is better suited to fan-out reactions than to a single coordinated workflow [[Event Bus#MediatR Notification Bus]].

cases.query: What does a custom IEventBus add beyond MediatR's default publish behavior, and why might you choose it?

expected.heading: Custom Event Bus
expected.text: When you need control over execution strategy

expected.heading: MediatR Notification Bus
expected.text: the container resolves all handlers at publish time

expected.heading: Event Bus
expected.text: The bus resolves handlers at runtime and invokes all of them when an event arrives

cases.referenceAnswer: A custom `IEventBus` adds explicit control over execution semantics such as parallel versus sequential execution, ordering, and error isolation [[Event Bus#Custom Event Bus]]. MediatR's publish model already resolves all handlers at publish time, but the notes say its default configuration does not expose that level of control [[Event Bus#MediatR Notification Bus]] [[Event Bus#Custom Event Bus]]. You would choose the custom bus when you need those semantics to be part of the implementation rather than left to framework defaults [[Event Bus#Custom Event Bus]].

cases.query: How does the Composite pattern let a pricing system treat single products and bundles uniformly, and what problem does it solve compared with a service that has separate code paths for each item type?

expected.heading: Composite
expected.text: The Composite pattern composes objects into tree structures and lets clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly through a shared interface.

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `PricingService` has separate logic for individual products, simple bundles, and nested bundles.

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Define `IOrderComponent` — both `SingleProduct` and `ProductBundle` implement it.

cases.referenceAnswer: Composite solves the pricing problem by moving the recursive structure into the objects themselves instead of keeping type checks and recursion in a service. In the problem example, `PricingService` has separate logic for products and bundles, which makes adding new item types awkward [[Composite#Problem]]. The solution is to define a shared component interface so both `SingleProduct` and `ProductBundle` can be treated uniformly, while the bundle recursively delegates to its children [[Composite#Solution]] [[Composite#Composite]].

cases.query: What is the difference between encapsulation and abstraction in OOP, and how do they each reduce complexity?

expected.heading: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
expected.text: The four pillars — encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, and polymorphism — are tools for managing complexity, not goals in themselves.

expected.heading: Encapsulation
expected.text: Hide internal state behind a controlled API so callers cannot put the object into an invalid state.

expected.heading: Abstraction
expected.text: Model only the contract a caller needs and hide everything else behind that boundary.

cases.referenceAnswer: Encapsulation and abstraction both reduce complexity, but they do so in different ways. Encapsulation protects an object’s internal state behind a controlled API so callers cannot put it into an invalid state [[OOP#Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)]] [[OOP#Encapsulation]]. Abstraction, by contrast, exposes only the contract a caller needs and hides the rest behind that boundary [[OOP#Abstraction]].

cases.query: How does the State pattern avoid large switch statements in an order workflow, and what role do the state objects play in transitions?

expected.heading: State
expected.text: The State pattern extracts state-specific behavior into separate state classes.

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: Every method repeats the same switch — adding a new status means editing all methods

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Each state becomes a class that knows its valid transitions:

cases.referenceAnswer: The State pattern removes repeated status switches by moving behavior into separate state classes [[State#State]] [[State#Problem]]. Instead of `Order` deciding every rule in a big switch, the current state object handles the operation and knows which transitions are valid [[State#Solution]]. The state objects also drive the transition itself, so the object decides its next state rather than the caller [[State#State]].

cases.query: In OOP, how do the four pillars relate to managing complexity, and which specific problem does polymorphism solve?

expected.heading: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
expected.text: The four pillars — encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, and polymorphism — are tools for managing complexity, not goals in themselves.

cases.referenceAnswer: The four pillars are presented as tools for managing complexity rather than ends in themselves [[OOP#Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)]]. Each pillar addresses a different kind of problem, and polymorphism specifically replaces many type-based conditionals with dispatch [[OOP#Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)]].

cases.query: How does the Chain of Responsibility show up in common .NET libraries, and what makes those APIs fit the pattern?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **ASP.NET Core Middleware pipeline** — the canonical .NET Chain of Responsibility.

cases.referenceAnswer: The Chain of Responsibility appears in ASP.NET Core middleware, `HttpClient` message handlers, and MediatR pipeline behaviors. In each case, a component handles the request and either calls the next link or short-circuits the chain. These examples all fit the pattern because responsibility is passed along a pipeline until one component handles it or the chain ends. [[Chain of Responsibility#You Already Use This]]

cases.query: What problems can happen in a Chain of Responsibility pipeline, and what practices help avoid them?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: The order of handlers is a business rule.

cases.referenceAnswer: A chain can fail when handlers are ordered incorrectly, when no handler ever processes the request, or when async handlers swallow exceptions. The section recommends treating handler order as a business rule and enforcing it in the composition root. It also says to always include a terminal handler or default behavior, and to decide up front whether the chain communicates success through return values or by throwing exceptions. [[Chain of Responsibility#Pitfalls]]

cases.query: When should you choose Chain of Responsibility instead of a monolithic method, and what tradeoffs does it have?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: | Concern | Chain of Responsibility | Monolithic method | |---|---|---| | Adding a new check | New handler class, zero changes | Edit existing method | | Handler ordering | Explicit at composition | I

cases.referenceAnswer: Chain of Responsibility is a good fit when you have three or more handlers, the set of handlers changes over time, or you want each handler to be tested independently. Compared with a monolithic method, it makes adding new checks easier and keeps short-circuiting explicit, but tracing a request can be less direct. For one or two fixed checks, the simpler method is usually less overhead. [[Chain of Responsibility#Tradeoffs]]

cases.query: How do GoF pattern categories help classify a pattern like Chain of Responsibility, and what is the main idea behind GoF patterns?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Behavioral if the problem is about assigning responsibility or defining the communication flow between objects.

expected.heading: GoF Design Patterns
expected.text: GoF (Gang of Four) Design Patterns are 23 reusable solutions to recurring object-oriented design problems, first documented in *Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software* (1994) b

cases.referenceAnswer: GoF patterns are 23 reusable solutions to recurring object-oriented design problems, and they are meant to provide a shared vocabulary for design intent. A pattern is classified as behavioral when the problem is about assigning responsibility or defining communication flow between objects. The category is about intent, so the key question is whether the design is about making objects, assembling objects, or communicating between objects. [[Design Patterns#GoF Design Patterns]] [[Design Patterns#Questions]]

cases.query: How does the discount rule DSL solve the hardcoded discount logic problem, and what runtime interpreter examples does the note compare it to?

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `DiscountService` has hardcoded if/else chains for discount rules — every new promotion requires code changes and deployment: ```csharp public class DiscountService { // ⚠️ Every new promotion rule re

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: A discount rule DSL where rules are stored as strings and evaluated at runtime

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **LINQ Expression Trees + EF Core `IQueryable<T>`** — the canonical production .NET Interpreter.

cases.referenceAnswer: The problem is that `DiscountService` hardcodes discount rules, so every new promotion requires a code change and deployment [[Interpreter#Problem]]. The solution is to move rules into a DSL that is stored as strings and evaluated at runtime, using expression objects like `OrderTotalGreaterThan` and `CustomerTierEquals` [[Interpreter#Solution]]. The note also points to LINQ expression trees with EF Core as a production example of the Interpreter pattern, where EF Core interprets the expression tree into SQL [[Interpreter#You Already Use This]].

cases.query: What makes ASP.NET Core middleware different from a classical Chain of Responsibility, and why is it considered a more performant but less flexible pipeline?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: ASP.NET Core Middleware uses a delegate pipeline

expected.heading: Chain of Responsibility
expected.text: The Chain of Responsibility pattern passes a request along a chain of handlers

cases.referenceAnswer: Classical Chain of Responsibility uses linked handlers where each handler points to the next, while ASP.NET Core middleware uses a delegate pipeline that is compiled at startup into a single delegate chain [[Chain of Responsibility#Questions]]. Because the pipeline is immutable after build, it does not support dynamic handler insertion the way a classical chain can, but it avoids virtual dispatch per handler and is therefore more performant [[Chain of Responsibility#Questions]]. The underlying pattern is still Chain of Responsibility: a request is passed along until a handler processes it or forwards it onward [[Chain of Responsibility#Chain of Responsibility]].

cases.query: How do the design-pattern notes explain when to choose Creational, Structural, or Behavioral, and what tradeoff do they mention about pattern categories?

expected.heading: The Three Categories
expected.text: **[[Software Engineering/05 Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Creational|Creational Patterns]]** (5 patterns) abstract the instantiation process, making systems independent of how objec

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: The category signals intent, not class structure

cases.referenceAnswer: Creational patterns are for object construction, Structural patterns are for composing classes or adapting interfaces, and Behavioral patterns are for algorithms and communication between objects [[Design Patterns#The Three Categories]]. The note says to choose based on intent: ask whether the problem is about making objects, assembling objects, or communicating between them [[Design Patterns#Questions]]. It also warns that category is about intent rather than structure, since patterns can look structurally similar while serving different purposes [[Design Patterns#Questions]].

cases.query: How does the Command pattern decouple request handling from execution in the restaurant example, and what capabilities does the note say commands enable?

expected.heading: Command
expected.text: A restaurant order ticket is a Command.

cases.referenceAnswer: In the restaurant example, the order ticket is the command: the waiter carries the request, the chef performs the work, and neither side needs to know the other’s full responsibilities [[Command#Command]]. This decouples who requests from who executes [[Command#Command]]. The note also says commands can be serialized, queued, logged, retried, and replayed because they are objects [[Command#Command]].

cases.query: How does the Observer pattern show up in common .NET APIs, and what are the main pitfalls to watch for when using events or observers?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: Every `event` in .NET is an Observer pattern implementation.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: if a short-lived subscriber subscribes to a long-lived publisher's event and never unsubscribes, the publisher holds a reference to the subscriber, preventing garbage collection.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Use C# `event` for synchronous, UI-oriented notifications where `async void` is acceptable (WPF/MAUI event handlers).

cases.referenceAnswer: In .NET, `event`/`delegate`, `IObservable<T>`/`IObserver<T>`, `INotifyPropertyChanged`, and `ObservableCollection<T>` are all examples of the Observer pattern [[Observer#You Already Use This]]. A key pitfall is forgetting to unsubscribe, which can keep short-lived subscribers alive and cause memory leaks [[Observer#Pitfalls]]. Another tradeoff is that `event` works best for synchronous UI-style notifications, while explicit observer interfaces are better when async behavior, error handling, or ordering matter [[Observer#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: When should I use C# events versus an explicit observer interface or Rx, and what tradeoffs drive that choice?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: Rx adds operators (filter, transform, combine) over the observable stream.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Use explicit `IObserver` interface for async, server-side notifications where error handling and ordering matter.

cases.referenceAnswer: Use C# `event` for synchronous, UI-oriented notifications where `async void` is acceptable, and use an explicit observer interface when you need async handling, better error control, or ordering guarantees [[Observer#Tradeoffs]]. If you need stream processing features like filtering, throttling, or combining values, `IObservable<T>` with Rx is the better fit because it adds operators over the stream [[Observer#You Already Use This]] [[Observer#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: What are the canonical .NET examples of the Chain of Responsibility pattern, and how does the pattern behave at runtime?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **ASP.NET Core Middleware pipeline** — the canonical .NET Chain of Responsibility.

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Handlers are composable and independently testable:

cases.referenceAnswer: ASP.NET Core middleware is the canonical .NET example of Chain of Responsibility, and each middleware either calls `await next(context)` to continue or returns early to short-circuit [[Chain of Responsibility#You Already Use This]]. The same idea appears in `HttpClient` `DelegatingHandler` pipelines and MediatR pipeline behaviors, where each link can pass control onward or stop the chain [[Chain of Responsibility#You Already Use This]]. In the validation example, the handlers are designed to be composable and independently testable [[Chain of Responsibility#Solution]].

cases.query: How does the Command pattern help with undo and auditability in an order workflow, and what .NET APIs already use this pattern?

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Each operation becomes a command object with `ExecuteAsync()` and `UndoAsync()`:

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: UI buttons bind to commands; the command encapsulates the action and its preconditions.

cases.referenceAnswer: The Command pattern turns each operation into an object with `ExecuteAsync()` and `UndoAsync()`, which makes rollback possible and lets the command carry a description for an audit trail [[Command#Solution]]. In .NET UI frameworks, `ICommand` uses the same idea: buttons bind to commands, and the command encapsulates both the action and its preconditions [[Command#You Already Use This]]. MediatR `IRequest<T>` plus a handler is another common Command pattern example in .NET [[Command#You Already Use This]].

cases.query: How does the State pattern compare to an enum plus switch, and when should I choose it?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: This is the State pattern at the language level.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Use State when you have 4+ states and 3+ operations, and the valid transitions differ significantly per state.

cases.referenceAnswer: The State pattern localizes behavior in separate state classes, while an enum plus switch keeps behavior in one place but spreads switch logic across methods. The note recommends State when you have 4+ states and 3+ operations and the valid transitions differ significantly per state; for 2-3 states with simple transitions, enum plus switch is less overhead. In other words, choose State when you are repeatedly copying the same switch statement into multiple methods. [[State#You Already Use This]] [[State#Tradeoffs]]

cases.query: What are the main pitfalls of the State pattern, and how should state transitions and persistence be handled?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: State transitions often have side effects (send email, trigger refund, update inventory).

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: The signal is when you find yourself copying the same switch statement into multiple methods.

cases.referenceAnswer: A key pitfall is state explosion, where many states and operations create a large number of methods, so a default base class or a declarative library can help reduce boilerplate. Another pitfall is missing transitions: side effects like refunds or inventory updates should live in the transition logic, not in the context. If the object is persisted, store the state name as a string and reconstruct the state object on load rather than serializing the state object directly. [[State#Pitfalls]] [[State#Tradeoffs]]

cases.query: How does the Strategy pattern differ from the State pattern in how behavior is selected and changed?

expected.heading: Strategy
expected.text: You choose the strategy before you start driving; the app uses it to calculate the route.

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: The compiler implements the State pattern for you:

cases.referenceAnswer: Strategy is about choosing among interchangeable algorithms, usually by the client or context, and then delegating execution to the selected strategy. The note’s navigation example says you choose the strategy before driving, and the context uses it without knowing which variant it received. State, by contrast, is about an object changing its own behavior as it moves through internal states over time. [[Strategy#Strategy]] [[State#You Already Use This]]

cases.query: What are the tradeoffs and decision rule for using Chain of Responsibility versus a monolithic method?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Use Chain of Responsibility when you have 3+ handlers that may process a request, the set of handlers changes over time, or handlers need to be independently testable.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Document the intended order and enforce it in the composition root, not scattered across handler registrations.

cases.referenceAnswer: Chain of Responsibility is a good fit when you have 3+ handlers, the handler set changes over time, or you want each handler tested independently. Compared with a monolithic method, it makes adding a new check easier and keeps handler ordering explicit, but you must manage ordering carefully. The note’s rule of thumb is that if you are adding else if blocks to a validation or processing method, CoR is worth considering. [[Chain of Responsibility#Tradeoffs]] [[Chain of Responsibility#Pitfalls]]

cases.query: When is the Visitor pattern a better fit than using pattern matching for cart item calculations, and what tradeoff does it make with adding new item types versus new operations?

expected.heading: Visitor
expected.text: The Visitor pattern lets you add new operations to an object hierarchy without modifying the classes in that hierarchy.

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: **Pattern matching approach** (modern C# — use for simple cases): ```csharp // ✅ Pattern matching — no Visitor ceremony for simple type dispatch public static class CartCalculations { public static de

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: adding a new calculation means editing every method

cases.referenceAnswer: Visitor is a better fit when you expect to add new operations often but want to keep the element hierarchy stable, because it lets you add operations without changing the element classes [[Visitor#Visitor]]. In the cart example, the problem is that each new calculation forces edits across multiple methods, so Visitor centralizes that behavior instead [[Visitor#Problem]]. By contrast, the pattern matching approach is presented as the simpler choice for small cases, while Visitor is recommended when operations are frequent and the hierarchy is stable [[Visitor#Solution]].

cases.query: How does double dispatch work in the Visitor pattern, and why does it ensure the correct Visit overload runs for each concrete element type?

expected.heading: Visitor
expected.text: A museum audio guide is a Visitor.

cases.referenceAnswer: Each element exposes an Accept method that calls visitor.Visit(this), so the element first chooses the visitor entry point and then the concrete element type determines which overload is selected [[Visitor#Visitor]]. This two-step dispatch is what the note calls double dispatch, and it is what ensures the correct Visit overload runs for the concrete element type [[Visitor#Visitor]]. The visitor can then carry both the operation and any accumulated state while traversing the hierarchy [[Visitor#Visitor]].

cases.query: What are the main tradeoffs between the pattern matching approach and the Visitor approach for cart calculations, especially when adding a GiftCard item?

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: **Pattern matching approach** (modern C# — use for simple cases): ```csharp // ✅ Pattern matching — no Visitor ceremony for simple type dispatch public static class CartCalculations { public static de

expected.heading: Visitor
expected.text: Adding a new tour doesn’t require changing any exhibit; adding a new exhibit requires updating every tour.

cases.referenceAnswer: The pattern matching approach is recommended for simple cases and makes a new item like GiftCard require one new case in each switch expression [[Visitor#Solution]]. The Visitor analogy emphasizes the opposite tradeoff: it is easy to add new operations, but adding a new element requires updating every visitor or tour [[Visitor#Visitor]]. So if the hierarchy is stable and you expect many operations, Visitor is favored; if the logic is simple and local, pattern matching is the lighter option [[Visitor#Solution]].

cases.query: When should I use query translation or multi-query retrieval instead of a single embedding search, and how does RAG-Fusion change the merging step?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Query translation rewrites a user question into one or more retrieval-optimized variants before search.

expected.heading: Multi-Query
expected.text: The LLM generates N paraphrases of the same intent, each targeting a different vocabulary or framing angle.

expected.heading: RAG-Fusion
expected.text: RAG-Fusion extends multi-query with explicit rank fusion.

cases.referenceAnswer: Use query translation when the user’s phrasing is likely to differ from the document phrasing, because it rewrites the question into retrieval-optimized variants before search [[Query Translation#Intro]]. Multi-query retrieval then runs several paraphrases independently so each one can hit a different neighborhood in embedding space [[Query Translation#Multi-Query]]. RAG-Fusion goes a step further by merging the variant results with Reciprocal Rank Fusion instead of simple deduplication, rewarding documents that rank well across multiple phrasings [[Query Translation#RAG-Fusion]].

cases.query: How do fixed-size, recursive, and semantic chunking differ, and what retrieval symptoms suggest switching strategies?

expected.heading: How to Choose a Strategy
expected.text: Use retrieval failures, not preference, to pick a strategy.

expected.heading: Fixed-Size Chunking
expected.text: How it works: - Split every document into windows of N tokens (or characters) with a configurable overlap between adjacent windows.

expected.heading: Recursive Chunking
expected.text: This preserves the largest coherent units possible.

cases.referenceAnswer: The guidance is to choose chunking based on observed retrieval failures rather than preference [[Chunking#How to Choose a Strategy]]. Fixed-size chunking is the fastest and most predictable operationally, but it can split sentences or logical units because it uses uniform windows with optional overlap [[Chunking#Fixed-Size Chunking]]. Recursive chunking preserves the largest coherent units possible by splitting first on larger separators and only recursing when needed [[Chunking#Recursive Chunking]].

cases.query: How does hybrid retrieval combine vector and keyword search, and why can rank fusion outperform a simple merge?

expected.heading: Hybrid Retrieval — Vector + Keyword
expected.text: Hybrid retrieval is like running both a full-text search (`WHERE body @@ to_tsquery('error & 429')`) and a vector similarity search against the same query, then merging the two result sets.

expected.heading: RAG-Fusion
expected.text: This acts as an implicit relevance vote: agreement across phrasings is a stronger signal than high rank from one phrasing.

cases.referenceAnswer: Hybrid retrieval runs vector search and keyword search in parallel on the same query, then merges the two ranked lists into one candidate set [[Retrieval#Hybrid Retrieval — Vector + Keyword]]. Rank fusion can outperform a simple merge because it treats agreement across result lists as a stronger relevance signal than a high rank from only one list [[Query Translation#RAG-Fusion]]. In other words, documents that are consistently near the top across retrievers or query variants are favored over documents that are strong in only one view [[Retrieval#Hybrid Retrieval — Vector + Keyword]].

cases.query: What causes silent recall degradation in HNSW at scale, and how should it be detected?

expected.heading: Indexing and Filtering
expected.text: The vector index determines the latency-recall tradeoff for vector search:

expected.heading: Silent Recall Degradation at Scale
expected.text: HNSW recall degrades as the corpus grows — no errors, no latency spike, just worse context fed to the LLM.

cases.referenceAnswer: HNSW exposes a latency-recall tradeoff, and its recall can silently worsen as the corpus grows if `ef_search` is not re-tuned [[Retrieval#Indexing and Filtering]]. The degradation is subtle because there may be no errors or latency spike, only worse context fed to the LLM [[Retrieval#Silent Recall Degradation at Scale]]. The recommended detection method is to maintain ground-truth query-chunk pairs and run scheduled Recall@k checks, since latency and error-rate monitoring alone will miss the regression [[Retrieval#Silent Recall Degradation at Scale]].

cases.query: When should you use a Strategy interface instead of a Func<T, TResult> delegate, and what are the tradeoffs?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **`IComparer<T>` / `IComparable<T>`** — comparison strategies.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- When should you use a Strategy interface vs a `Func<T, TResult>` delegate? > Use a `Func<T, TResult>` delegate when the strategy is simple (one method, no state, no dependencies).

cases.referenceAnswer: Use a Func<T, TResult> delegate when the strategy is simple, with one method and no state or dependencies. Use an interface when the strategy needs multiple methods, DI-injected dependencies, DI registration, or state. The tradeoff is that delegates are simpler and more flexible, while interfaces are more explicit and support DI. [[Strategy#You Already Use This]] [[Strategy#Questions]]

cases.query: How do LINQ expression trees and EF Core illustrate the Interpreter pattern?

expected.heading: Interpreter
expected.text: A calculator is an Interpreter.

expected.heading: References
expected.text: [Expression Trees — Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/csharp/advanced-topics/expression-trees/) — LINQ expression trees as the canonical .NET Interpreter

cases.referenceAnswer: The Interpreter pattern defines a grammar and an interpreter that evaluates sentences in that grammar. In the note, LINQ expression trees are described as an abstract syntax tree that EF Core interprets into SQL at runtime, without you writing SQL. The references also identify expression trees as the canonical .NET Interpreter. [[Interpreter#Interpreter]] [[Interpreter#References]]

cases.query: What examples show Strategy being used in .NET for comparison, filtering, serialization, and password hashing?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **`IComparer<T>` / `IComparable<T>`** — comparison strategies.

cases.referenceAnswer: The note gives several .NET examples of Strategy: IComparer<T> and IComparable<T> for comparison, LINQ OrderBy with a Func<T, TKey> as an inline strategy, LINQ Where with a Func<T, bool> predicate as a filtering strategy, JsonConverter<T> for serialization, and IPasswordHasher<T> for password hashing. It also notes that swapping the hasher is a strategy swap. [[Strategy#You Already Use This]]

cases.query: How does the Iterator pattern help avoid loading an entire order history into memory, and what C# features implement it?

expected.heading: Iterator
expected.text: The Iterator pattern provides a way to sequentially access elements of a collection without exposing its underlying representation.

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `OrderRepository` returns a `List<Order>` for the entire order history — memory explosion for customers with thousands of orders:

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Use `IEnumerable<T>` with `yield return` for lazy, paginated iteration:

cases.referenceAnswer: The Iterator pattern lets you access items one at a time without exposing how the collection is stored, which avoids loading everything up front [[Iterator]]. In the order-history example, returning a full `List<Order>` can cause memory explosion for customers with many orders, while the solution uses lazy, paginated iteration so the caller only consumes what it needs [[Iterator#Problem]] [[Iterator#Solution]]. In C#, `foreach` and `yield return` are the Iterator pattern, with `IEnumerable<T>` as the iterable and `IEnumerator<T>` as the iterator [[Iterator]].

cases.query: What is the relationship between `IEnumerable<T>`, `IEnumerator<T>`, `foreach`, and `yield return` in C#?

expected.heading: Iterator
expected.text: In C#, **`foreach` and `yield return` ARE the Iterator pattern** — the compiler generates the iterator state machine (`IEnumerator<T>`) for you.

cases.referenceAnswer: In C#, `foreach` and `yield return` are the Iterator pattern, and the compiler generates the iterator state machine for you [[Iterator]]. `IEnumerable<T>` is the iterable that knows how to create an iterator, while `IEnumerator<T>` is the iterator that tracks position [[Iterator]].

cases.query: Why is the Interpreter pattern used for LINQ queries in EF Core, and what are some other examples of interpreters mentioned?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **LINQ Expression Trees + EF Core `IQueryable<T>`** — the canonical production .NET Interpreter.

cases.referenceAnswer: EF Core uses LINQ expression trees as a grammar and interprets them into SQL, so `IQueryable<T>` with expression trees is described as the canonical production .NET Interpreter [[Interpreter#You Already Use This]]. The note says the expression tree is the grammar and EF Core is the interpreter, which is why unsupported expressions can fail translation [[Interpreter#You Already Use This]]. It also gives `Regex` as another interpreter example, where the regex engine interprets the pattern against the input string [[Interpreter#You Already Use This]].

cases.query: How does MediatR compare to SignalR hub context and message buses as mediator examples, and what do they have in common?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **MediatR `IMediator`** — the canonical .NET Mediator.

cases.referenceAnswer: MediatR, SignalR hub context, and message buses are all examples of the mediator pattern in different parts of a .NET system. MediatR routes commands to handlers, SignalR broadcasts from server code to connected clients, and message buses route published events to consumers without the sender knowing who the recipients are [[Mediator#You Already Use This]].

cases.query: When does the mediator pattern become a bottleneck or anti-pattern, and what is a clearer alternative for simple interactions?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: When the mediator becomes a god class that knows too much

cases.referenceAnswer: Mediator becomes an anti-pattern when it turns into a god class that accumulates too much logic, because it only reduces coupling and does not reduce complexity [[Mediator#Questions]]. For simple one-to-one interactions, a direct dependency is clearer than routing through a mediator [[Mediator#Questions]].

cases.query: How do MediatR pipeline behaviors relate to the Chain of Responsibility pattern?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Each `IPipelineBehavior<TRequest, TResponse>` wraps the next behavior in the pipeline.

cases.referenceAnswer: MediatR pipeline behaviors implement a Chain of Responsibility style pipeline because each behavior wraps the next one and decides whether to continue or stop early [[Mediator#Questions]]. In other words, `Handle(request, next, ct)` can either pass control onward by calling `next()` or short-circuit the request [[Mediator#Questions]].

cases.query: How does EF Core use LINQ expression trees as an Interpreter, and what happens when a query contains an unsupported custom method?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: the C# compiler doesn't compile the lambda to IL — it compiles it to an `Expression<Func<Order, bool>>`

cases.referenceAnswer: EF Core treats the LINQ expression tree as the grammar and walks it with `ExpressionVisitor` to translate nodes into SQL [[Interpreter#Questions]]. Because it can only interpret expressions it knows, a custom method inside the query can fail with `InvalidOperationException` when the translator has no rule for that method [[Interpreter#Questions]].

cases.query: How does the Memento pattern let a shopping cart support undo without exposing its internal state, and what roles do the originator and caretaker play?

expected.heading: Memento
expected.text: The Memento pattern captures and externalizes an object’s internal state so it can be restored later, without violating encapsulation.

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: `CartMemento` captures cart state; `CartHistory` stores snapshots: ```csharp // Memento — immutable snapshot of cart state public sealed record CartMemento( IReadOnlyList<CartItem> Items, string? Disc

cases.referenceAnswer: The Memento pattern lets an object save a snapshot of its state so it can be restored later without breaking encapsulation [[Memento#Memento]]. In this example, the shopping cart is the originator: it creates the memento, and the history manager is the caretaker: it stores the snapshots without inspecting them [[Memento#Memento]]. The cart can later restore itself from a saved memento to support undo [[Memento#Memento]].

cases.query: What problem does the shopping cart example have before using Memento, and why is that a poor fit for undo or recovery?

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: A shopping cart has no undo.

cases.referenceAnswer: Before using Memento, the shopping cart has no undo, so removing an item by accident is permanent [[Memento#Problem]]. Abandoned cart recovery also depends on external database snapshots, which means there is no clean abstraction for restoring prior state [[Memento#Problem]]. That makes changing requirements like undo especially awkward because the cart has no built-in snapshot capability [[Memento#Problem]].

cases.query: What state does the cart memento capture, and what does the cart history store?

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: `CartMemento` captures cart state; `CartHistory` stores snapshots: ```csharp // Memento — immutable snapshot of cart state public sealed record CartMemento( IReadOnlyList<CartItem> Items, string? Disc

cases.referenceAnswer: The cart memento captures the cart’s state as an immutable snapshot, and the history component stores those snapshots [[Memento#Solution]]. The example shows the cart state including items and discount code, with the memento defined as a record that holds that snapshot data [[Memento#Solution]]. This lets the cart save and later restore a prior state without exposing its internal list directly [[Memento#Solution]].

cases.query: How does the Strategy pattern let a navigation app switch between fastest, shortest, toll-free, and scenic routing without changing the navigation engine?

expected.heading: Strategy
expected.text: A navigation app uses the Strategy pattern every time you pick a route.

cases.referenceAnswer: The navigation app can treat each routing algorithm as a separate strategy and choose one before or during the trip. The app keeps the navigation engine unchanged while swapping between algorithms like fastest, shortest, avoid tolls, or scenic. This is an example of the Strategy pattern, where the context delegates the algorithm to an interchangeable strategy [[Strategy#Strategy]].

cases.query: What problem does the shipping cost example illustrate, and how does the strategy-based solution avoid editing a growing if/else chain when adding new shipping methods?

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `ShippingService.CalculateCost()` has an if/else chain — adding a new strategy means editing the method: ```csharp public class ShippingService { // ⚠️ Adding "same-day delivery" requires editing this

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Each algorithm becomes a strategy class.

cases.referenceAnswer: The problem is that `ShippingService.CalculateCost()` uses an if/else chain, so adding a new shipping method requires editing the method itself [[Strategy#Problem]]. The solution is to turn each algorithm into its own strategy class and let the context select among them via DI or a registry [[Strategy#Solution]]. That way, adding a new algorithm means adding a new strategy class instead of changing a growing switch statement [[Strategy#Solution]].

cases.query: How does the State pattern explain an order changing behavior after moving from Pending to Paid, and how is that different from the caller controlling the transition?

expected.heading: State
expected.text: Think of a vending machine.

cases.referenceAnswer: In the State pattern, the `Order` context delegates behavior to its current state object, so the same call can behave differently depending on whether the order is Pending or Paid [[State#State]]. When the order transitions from Pending to Paid, the context swaps its state object and `Ship()` changes behavior without a switch statement [[State#State]]. The key difference is that the state object decides the next state, not the caller [[State#State]].

cases.query: How does the async/await compiler use a state machine to resume execution after each await, and why is that considered the State pattern?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- How does the `async`/`await` compiler implement the State pattern? > The compiler transforms an `async` method into a struct implementing `IAsyncStateMachine`.

cases.referenceAnswer: The compiler turns an `async` method into a struct that implements `IAsyncStateMachine`, and it stores the current position in a `state` field [[State#Questions]]. `MoveNext()` switches on that state so execution can resume from the last `await` point after suspension [[State#Questions]]. This is considered the State pattern because the method’s execution state drives behavior and transitions happen automatically at each `await` [[State#Questions]].

cases.query: How does the Template Method pattern help remove duplicated orchestration logic in the report generators, and which steps stay fixed versus customized?

In the example, the fixed steps are fetch, validate, and audit, while formatting and output details vary by subclass [[Template Method]].

cases.query: How should I monitor a RAG system so that latency improvements don't hide quality regressions, especially when cached responses or provider model changes are involved?

expected.heading: Monitoring Only Latency While Quality Degrades
expected.text: Latency-only SLOs create a false sense of health.

cases.referenceAnswer: You should not rely on latency alone, because a system can meet latency SLOs while serving increasingly ungrounded answers. This can happen if a provider silently substitutes a faster but less accurate model, or if cache hit rates rise while cached responses become stale. The fix is to pair latency metrics with sampled quality metrics so the dashboard reflects both speed and answer faithfulness [[Monitoring#Monitoring Only Latency While Quality Degrades]].

cases.query: How do I detect when an LLM judge has drifted over time, and what threshold should trigger recalibration?

expected.heading: Judge Drift Without Calibration
expected.text: The LLM judge used for production scoring drifts over time

expected.heading: Judge Prompt Design
expected.text: The judge prompt is the most important lever.

cases.referenceAnswer: Treat the judge like a monitored test harness: keep a small calibration set of 50–100 human-labeled examples and run the judge against it weekly. Track judge-human agreement over time, and if it falls below 80%, recalibrate the prompt or switch to another judge model. A well-structured judge prompt matters because it locks in the rubric and reference context the judge uses to score groundedness [[Monitoring#Judge Drift Without Calibration]] [[LLM-as-a-Judge#Judge Prompt Design]].

cases.query: Why should alerts be fired at the segment level in multi-tenant RAG monitoring instead of only using global aggregates?

expected.heading: Alerting on Global Aggregates Instead of Segments
expected.text: The most common monitoring failure in multi-tenant RAG.

cases.referenceAnswer: Global aggregates can hide a serious problem in one tenant or segment. In the example, global faithfulness stays at 0.92 while one tenant drops to 0.68, so the alert never fires even though that tenant is failing. The recommended fix is to alert at the segment level, and if that creates too much noise, use a tiered system with immediate alerts for high-priority segments and daily digests for low-priority ones [[Monitoring#Alerting on Global Aggregates Instead of Segments]].

cases.query: How do I evaluate chunking quality in a RAG pipeline, and what metrics should I use to judge whether retrieved chunks cover the needed evidence efficiently?

expected.heading: Chunking Evaluation
expected.text: There is no standalone "chunking quality" metric in most RAG frameworks.

cases.referenceAnswer: Chunking quality is evaluated through its downstream impact on retrieval rather than with a standalone metric. Build `(query, gold_evidence_span)` pairs and compare retrieved chunks against the gold evidence using token-level recall, precision, and especially Token IoU, which measures how efficiently the retrieved chunks cover the needed evidence while penalizing noise. Token IoU is more informative than end-to-end Recall@k because it captures chunk efficiency [[RAG Evaluation#Chunking Evaluation]].

cases.query: How does re-ranking differ from first-stage retrieval, and why is it used before the generator?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Retrieval optimizes for recall at speed — find plausible candidates from millions of chunks.

cases.referenceAnswer: Re-ranking is a second-stage scoring pass that takes the candidate set from retrieval and reorders it with a more expensive, more accurate model before the generator sees the context [[Re-ranking#Intro]]. First-stage retrieval is optimized for recall and returns a broad candidate set, while re-ranking is optimized for precision and pushes the most relevant items to the top [[Re-ranking#Intro]].

cases.query: Why are cross-encoders well-suited for reranking but impractical for first-stage retrieval?

expected.heading: Cross-Encoder Reranking
expected.text: A cross-encoder takes the query and a single document as a concatenated input, passes them through a transformer together, and outputs a relevance score.

cases.referenceAnswer: A cross-encoder scores a query-document pair jointly, which lets it capture fine-grained interactions such as negation, qualifier scope, and entity co-reference [[Re-ranking#Cross-Encoder Reranking]]. Because it must run once per query-document pair, scoring even a small candidate set requires many forward passes, so it is too slow for first-stage retrieval over millions of chunks [[Re-ranking#Cross-Encoder Reranking]]. That same cost profile makes it a good fit for reranking a small set of candidates [[Re-ranking#Cross-Encoder Reranking]].

cases.query: How does ColBERT's late interaction compare with cross-encoder reranking in terms of scoring and efficiency?

expected.heading: Cross-Encoder Reranking
expected.text: This joint encoding captures fine-grained interactions — negation, qualifier scope, entity co-reference — that independent embeddings miss.

expected.heading: Late Interaction — ColBERT
expected.text: ColBERT (Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT) encodes query and document independently into per-token embeddings, then scores relevance using MaxSim:

cases.referenceAnswer: Cross-encoders score a query and document together, which gives them full token-level attention and lets them capture fine-grained interactions like negation and co-reference [[Re-ranking#Cross-Encoder Reranking]]. ColBERT instead encodes query and document independently into per-token embeddings and uses MaxSim to score token-level alignment [[Re-ranking#Late Interaction — ColBERT]]. Its main efficiency advantage is that document embeddings are pre-computed and stored at index time, so at query time only the query needs encoding [[Re-ranking#Late Interaction — ColBERT]].

cases.query: When should a RAG system use hybrid retrieval instead of vector-only search for technical support questions with error codes or version strings?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: | Mode | Recall profile | Latency | Operational complexity | Best for | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Vector only | Strong on semantic paraphrases -- weak on exact identifiers | Low -- single inde

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Technical support queries often include exact identifiers: error codes, version strings, API paths, and SKU IDs.

expected.heading: BM25 Lexical Reranking
expected.text: The strength is precision on named entities, error codes, product SKUs, legal terms, and acronyms.

cases.referenceAnswer: Hybrid retrieval is a good default when queries mix semantic language with exact identifiers, because vector-only search is weak on exact tokens while keyword methods are strong on them [[Retrieval#Tradeoffs]]. Technical support questions often contain error codes, version strings, API paths, and SKU IDs, so a hybrid approach helps avoid missing the exact match [[Retrieval#Questions]]. BM25 is especially useful when exact terminology matters, including error codes and product SKUs [[Re-ranking#BM25 Lexical Reranking]].

cases.query: Why can vector-only retrieval underperform on technical support workloads, and how does BM25 help?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: | Mode | Recall profile | Latency | Operational complexity | Best for | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Vector only | Strong on semantic paraphrases -- weak on exact identifiers | Low -- single inde

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Embedding models capture meaning, not specific tokens

expected.heading: BM25 Lexical Reranking
expected.text: BM25 scores a document by matching query terms against document terms

cases.referenceAnswer: Vector-only retrieval can underperform because it is good at semantic paraphrases but weak on exact identifiers, so it may miss the specific token a support query needs [[Retrieval#Tradeoffs]]. Technical support queries often include rare tokens like error codes and version strings, and embedding models capture meaning rather than those exact tokens [[Retrieval#Questions]]. BM25 helps by matching query terms directly against document terms, which improves precision for exact terminology [[Re-ranking#BM25 Lexical Reranking]].

cases.query: How should you choose an embedding model when your corpus includes non-English queries and domain-specific terminology?

expected.heading: Multilingual Embedding Collapse
expected.text: Models trained primarily on English text cluster non-English content into a smaller region of vector space

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: | Factor | Proprietary API | Open-Source Self-Hosted | Domain-Finetuned | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Cost at scale | Per-token pricing -- scales linearly | Infrastructure cost -- GPU amortized across

cases.referenceAnswer: If your corpus includes non-English queries, you should prefer models with explicit multilingual training because English-centric models can collapse distinct non-English concepts into a smaller region of vector space [[Embeddings#Multilingual Embedding Collapse]]. If your corpus also contains domain-specific terminology, general-purpose models can miss internal terms, while domain-finetuned models have the highest recall on domain text because they are trained on your distribution [[Embeddings#Tradeoffs]]. In practice, that means multilingual support and domain adaptation address different failure modes and may both matter [[Embeddings#Multilingual Embedding Collapse]] [[Embeddings#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: Why is MTEB not enough by itself for selecting an embedding model for RAG?

expected.heading: Benchmark Leaderboard Overfitting
expected.text: MTEB aggregates scores across multiple task families

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: | Factor | Proprietary API | Open-Source Self-Hosted | Domain-Finetuned | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Cost at scale | Per-token pricing -- scales linearly | Infrastructure cost -- GPU amortized across

cases.referenceAnswer: MTEB alone is not enough because it aggregates results across many task families, so a model can look strong overall while still performing poorly on retrieval-specific datasets [[Embeddings#Benchmark Leaderboard Overfitting]]. For RAG, you should filter MTEB to the Retrieval task category and validate on your own evaluation set [[Embeddings#Benchmark Leaderboard Overfitting]]. General-purpose models may still have high recall on general text, but that does not guarantee they are the best choice for your retrieval workload [[Embeddings#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: How do vector databases support dense retrieval at scale, and what tradeoff does approximate nearest-neighbor search make compared with exact search?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A vector database stores [[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/LLM/Embeddings|embeddings]] alongside their metadata and serves nearest-neighbor search over them at scale.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The vector is matched against pre-indexed chunk vectors in a vector database.

expected.heading: Dense Retrieval — Vector Search
expected.text: How it works: - An embedding model converts the query and each document chunk into fixed-size vectors in the same space.

cases.referenceAnswer: Vector databases store embeddings with metadata and provide nearest-neighbor search at scale, which is the infrastructure used for dense retrieval [[Vector Databases#Intro]] [[Retrieval#Dense Retrieval — Vector Search]]. They use approximate nearest-neighbor search rather than exact search, so they trade a small amount of recall for much faster lookup over large collections [[Vector Databases#Intro]].

cases.query: When should you use HNSW versus flat brute-force indexing, and what HNSW parameters control the recall-latency tradeoff?

expected.heading: Index Types
expected.text: The index is the core choice; it sets the recall, latency, memory, and build-time profile.

cases.referenceAnswer: Flat indexing compares against every vector, so it is exact but only practical for small collections or for measuring ANN recall against ground truth [[Vector Databases#Index Types]]. HNSW is the default choice in many vector databases because it offers high recall at low latency, but it is memory-hungry since the graph and vectors live in RAM [[Vector Databases#Index Types]]. Its main tuning knobs are M, ef_construction, and ef_search, with higher ef_search increasing recall and latency together [[Vector Databases#Index Types]].

cases.query: Why is pre-filtering important for tenant-safe retrieval, and why can post-filtering hurt recall under selective filters?

expected.heading: Metadata Filtering
expected.text: Real queries are rarely pure similarity — they are "similar *and* authorized, *and* in this date range, *and* this tenant." How filtering combines with ANN matters: - **Pre-filtering** narrows the can

cases.referenceAnswer: Pre-filtering is required when retrieval must be tenant-safe or ACL-scoped, because semantic similarity alone does not enforce authorization and the filter must limit which vectors are eligible before search [[Vector Databases#Metadata Filtering]]. Post-filtering is simpler, but if the filter is highly selective it can remove most of the ANN top-k results, shrinking the effective result set and collapsing recall [[Vector Databases#Metadata Filtering]].

cases.query: What are the main differences between webhooks, polling, SSE, and WebSockets, and when should each be used?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: You reach for webhooks when you need low-latency, push-based integration between systems that communicate over HTTP

expected.heading: Tradeoffs: Webhooks vs Polling vs SSE vs WebSockets
expected.text: | **Polling** | Pull (consumer to producer) | Interval-bound delay | Low | Stateless per-request | Simple integrations, systems without webhook support |

cases.referenceAnswer: Webhooks are a push-based HTTP integration pattern for low-latency server-to-server communication, especially across organizational boundaries where shared brokers are impractical [[Webhooks#Intro]]. Polling is simpler but introduces interval-bound delay, SSE is a good fit for one-directional browser notifications, and WebSockets are best when you need real-time bidirectional communication [[Webhooks#Tradeoffs: Webhooks vs Polling vs SSE vs WebSockets]].

cases.query: How do domain events, integration events, and event notifications differ, and what is the practical rule for modeling them?

expected.heading: Event Types
expected.text: **Domain Event** - Describes something meaningful that happened inside a bounded context.

expected.heading: Difference at a Glance
expected.text: | Domain Event | Capture domain fact | Rich domain data | Same bounded context |

cases.referenceAnswer: A domain event captures a meaningful fact inside a bounded context and is primarily for the same service or domain, while an integration event is a stable contract meant for other services to consume [[Event-Driven Architecture#Event Types]] [[Event-Driven Architecture#Difference at a Glance]]. An event notification is lighter weight and mainly signals that something changed, often with minimal metadata and with consumers re-querying for full state if needed [[Event-Driven Architecture#Event Types]] [[Event-Driven Architecture#Difference at a Glance]]. The practical rule is to model domain events first and then map only the externally relevant subset into integration events [[Event-Driven Architecture#Difference at a Glance]].

cases.query: What reliability patterns should be used for a webhook receiver that enqueues work for asynchronous processing?

expected.heading: Receiver Example (ASP.NET Core)
expected.text: A production-quality webhook receiver needs three things: signature verification, idempotency, and fast acknowledgment.

expected.heading: Reliability patterns
expected.text: - **Retry with backoff** - Retry transient failures with exponential backoff + jitter.

expected.heading: Concrete .NET example (Webhook -> Queue -> Worker)
expected.text: Common webhook pattern: acknowledge HTTP quickly, enqueue durable work, then process asynchronously with idempotent handlers.

cases.referenceAnswer: A production-quality webhook receiver should verify the signature, acknowledge quickly, and rely on idempotent processing after enqueueing durable work [[Webhooks#Receiver Example (ASP.NET Core)]] [[Message Queues#Concrete .NET example (Webhook -> Queue -> Worker)]]. For the asynchronous worker, retry transient failures with exponential backoff and jitter, and use durable idempotency keys so duplicate deliveries do not repeat side effects [[Message Queues#Reliability patterns]]. The overall pattern is to accept the webhook fast, publish work to a queue, and process it later with idempotent handlers [[Message Queues#Concrete .NET example (Webhook -> Queue -> Worker)]].

cases.query: When is event-driven architecture a better fit than synchronous APIs, and how does it relate to durable retained events?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a style where services communicate by publishing and consuming events instead of calling each other directly through synchronous APIs.

cases.referenceAnswer: Event-driven architecture is a good fit when services should communicate by publishing and consuming events rather than calling each other synchronously [[Event-Driven Architecture#Intro]]. It reduces runtime coupling, supports independent scaling, and improves resilience when a downstream component is temporarily unavailable [[Event-Driven Architecture#Intro]]. It is especially useful when workflows cross service boundaries, processing can be asynchronous, or you need durable retained events for audit or replay [[Event-Driven Architecture#Intro]].

cases.query: When should a .NET team choose Kafka over RabbitMQ, and what queue pitfalls should they watch for around ordering and duplicate side effects?

expected.heading: .NET platform choices
expected.text: Use [[Kafka]] for replayable event streams.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: teams assume global ordering and break business invariants.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Choose Kafka for replayable event streams with high throughput.

cases.referenceAnswer: Choose Kafka when you need replayable event streams with high throughput, and choose RabbitMQ when you need low-latency work queues and routing-key patterns [[Message Queues# .NET platform choices]] [[Message Queues#Questions]]. In queue-driven systems, you should not assume global ordering, because most brokers only guarantee order per partition and retries or competing consumers can reorder work [[Message Queues#Pitfalls]]. You also need idempotency, because at-least-once delivery can otherwise produce duplicate side effects such as charges, emails, or external calls [[Message Queues#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: How do you prevent loss and duplicate side effects in at-least-once queue processing, and what broker behavior makes that necessary?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: one bad message retries forever and starves healthy traffic.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Use manual ack only after successful business commit.

cases.referenceAnswer: To prevent loss and duplicate side effects, acknowledge a message only after the business commit succeeds, then rely on broker redelivery if the consumer crashes before the ack [[Message Queues#Questions]]. Because redelivery is expected, handlers must be idempotent and use an atomic idempotency reservation such as a unique key or transactional insert [[Message Queues#Questions]] [[Message Queues#Pitfalls]]. You should also bound retries and route persistent failures to a dead-letter queue so one poison message does not retry forever and starve healthy traffic [[Message Queues#Questions]] [[Message Queues#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: What are the main scaling patterns for a read-heavy relational workload, and what tradeoffs do they introduce?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Scalability is a system's ability to keep serving requests as load grows by adding resources, without a proportional drop in reliability or latency.

expected.heading: Core Patterns
expected.text: Database read replicas | Read-heavy relational load | Offload read queries from primary to replicas

cases.referenceAnswer: Scalability means keeping service quality as load grows by adding resources without a proportional drop in reliability or latency [[Scalability Patterns#Intro]]. For a read-heavy relational workload, database read replicas help by offloading read queries from the primary to replicas [[Scalability Patterns#Core Patterns]]. The main caveat is replica lag, which can break read-after-write expectations [[Scalability Patterns#Core Patterns]]. If the bottleneck is write throughput or dataset size instead, database sharding spreads writes and storage across shards, but it adds rebalancing and cross-shard complexity [[Scalability Patterns#Core Patterns]].

cases.query: What does an API gateway centralize, and why is it useful in a microservices system with multiple client types?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: An API Gateway is a single entry point between external clients and a set of backend services.

cases.referenceAnswer: An API gateway is a single entry point between external clients and backend services [[API Gateway#Intro]]. It centralizes cross-cutting concerns such as request routing, authentication and authorization enforcement, rate limiting, TLS termination, and traffic policies [[API Gateway#Intro]]. It is especially useful when you have microservices with multiple consumer types, because it keeps clients simpler and gives you one place to enforce consistency and security [[API Gateway#Intro]].

cases.query: How does CAP theorem define the tradeoff during a network partition, and what does it mean operationally for reads and writes?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: CAP theorem says that in a distributed data system, once a network partition happens, you can guarantee at most one of **strong consistency** or **availability** (while still tolerating the partition)

expected.heading: Definitions in operational terms
expected.text: The real forced choice is:

expected.heading: Mechanism: Why You Cannot Have C and A During Partition
expected.text: Imagine two replicas, `R1` and `R2`, serving the same key.

cases.referenceAnswer: During a network partition, CAP says you can guarantee at most one of strong consistency or availability while still tolerating the partition. Operationally, consistency means every successful read sees the most recent successful write, while availability means every request to a non-failed node gets a non-error response in finite time [[CAP theorem#Intro]] [[CAP theorem#Definitions in operational terms]]. The replica example shows the tradeoff: answering immediately can preserve availability but return stale data, while blocking or rejecting can preserve consistency but reduce availability [[CAP theorem#Mechanism: Why You Cannot Have C and A During Partition]].

cases.query: Why are message queues useful for handling spikes and downstream slowness, and how do their delivery guarantees affect design?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Message queues decouple producers from consumers by buffering messages until consumers are ready.

expected.heading: Core concepts
expected.text: - **Queue vs Topic** - `Queue` (point-to-point): one message is consumed by one worker in a competing-consumer group.

cases.referenceAnswer: Message queues are useful because they decouple producers from consumers by buffering work until consumers are ready, which helps absorb spikes and keep systems working when downstream services slow down [[Message Queues#Intro]]. Their delivery guarantees matter because at-least-once delivery can redeliver messages and produce duplicates, so consumers usually need idempotency or another way to make side effects safe [[Message Queues#Core concepts]].

cases.query: What is the difference between choreography and orchestration in event-driven architecture, and when would you choose each one?

expected.heading: Choreography
expected.text: In choreography, each service reacts to events independently.

expected.heading: Orchestration
expected.text: In orchestration, a central component (process manager/saga orchestrator) directs the workflow and issues commands.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: - **Choreography**: looser coupling and easier service autonomy, but harder to trace global flow and reason about emergent behavior as subscriptions grow.

cases.referenceAnswer: Choreography means each service reacts to events independently, with no central coordinator telling services what to do next [[Event-Driven Architecture#Choreography]]. Orchestration uses a central component to direct the workflow and issue commands, and it is preferred when workflow visibility, explicit state handling, and compensation logic matter [[Event-Driven Architecture#Orchestration]]. The tradeoff is that choreography improves autonomy but is harder to trace, while orchestration gives clearer process control at the cost of a central dependency [[Event-Driven Architecture#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: How do RabbitMQ exchange types differ, and which one would you use for command-style tasks versus broadcasting an event to multiple consumers?

expected.heading: AMQP Model
expected.text: RabbitMQ routing is explicit: producers publish to an exchange, not directly to a queue (except via the default exchange behavior).

expected.heading: Exchange Types
expected.text: Direct | Exact routing key match | Command-style task queues (`order.created`)

cases.referenceAnswer: RabbitMQ routes messages through exchanges, with bindings and routing keys determining which queues receive a message [[RabbitMQ#AMQP Model]]. For command-style task queues, a direct exchange is the typical choice because it matches an exact routing key [[RabbitMQ#Exchange Types]]. For broadcasting one event to many consumers, a fanout exchange is the right fit because it sends the message to all bound queues [[RabbitMQ#Exchange Types]].

cases.query: When should I choose RabbitMQ, Kafka, or Azure Service Bus for a .NET messaging system?

expected.heading: .NET platform choices
expected.text: Use [[RabbitMQ]] for routing-heavy queues and latency-sensitive tasks.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform built around an append-only commit log

cases.referenceAnswer: RabbitMQ is the best fit when you need routing-heavy queues or latency-sensitive tasks, such as background jobs, webhook pipelines, or command dispatch [[Message Queues#.NET platform choices]]. Kafka is the better choice for replayable event streams and other workloads that benefit from a durable append-only log and high throughput [[Message Queues#.NET platform choices]] [[Kafka#Intro]]. Azure Service Bus is the managed option when you want queues/topics and dead-lettering without operating the broker yourself [[Message Queues#.NET platform choices]].

cases.query: Why is RabbitMQ a good fit for a webhook-to-worker pipeline, and what problem does it solve?

expected.heading: Concrete .NET example (Webhook -> Queue -> Worker)
expected.text: Common webhook pattern: acknowledge HTTP quickly, enqueue durable work, then process asynchronously with idempotent handlers.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: In a `Webhook -> Queue -> Worker` system, RabbitMQ is usually the safety valve between bursty ingress and bounded worker throughput.

cases.referenceAnswer: A webhook-to-worker pipeline should acknowledge HTTP quickly, enqueue durable work, and then process it asynchronously with idempotent handlers [[Message Queues#Concrete .NET example (Webhook -> Queue -> Worker)]]. RabbitMQ fits well here because it decouples producers and consumers, enables asynchronous processing, and absorbs traffic spikes [[RabbitMQ#Intro]]. In that setup, RabbitMQ acts as the safety valve between bursty ingress and bounded worker throughput [[RabbitMQ#Intro]].

cases.query: How do idempotency keys make retries safe for non-idempotent API writes, and what server steps are involved?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: In distributed systems this is not optional because retries, timeouts, dropped acknowledgments, and at least once delivery are normal operating conditions, not edge cases.

expected.heading: Idempotency keys
expected.text: For non-idempotent operations, the client sends a unique key per logical operation in `Idempotency-Key`.

cases.referenceAnswer: Idempotency matters because retries, timeouts, dropped acknowledgments, and at-least-once delivery are normal, so a retried write must not create extra side effects [[Idempotency#Intro]]. For non-idempotent operations, the client includes a unique Idempotency-Key per logical operation, and the server checks a durable store for that key before deciding what to do [[Idempotency#Idempotency keys]]. If the key already exists and the request fingerprint matches, the server returns the stored response; otherwise it processes the request exactly once, persists the response, and binds it to the key [[Idempotency#Idempotency keys]].

cases.query: Why are HTTP PUT and DELETE considered idempotent, while POST and incrementing a balance are not?

expected.heading: Natural idempotency
expected.text: - `SET balance = 100` is idempotent because repeating it keeps the same result.

cases.referenceAnswer: An operation is idempotent if repeating it produces the same final state as applying it once [[Idempotency#Natural idempotency]]. Setting a value like SET balance = 100 is idempotent because repeating it keeps the same result, while INCREMENT balance by 100 is not because each retry changes state again [[Idempotency#Natural idempotency]]. By HTTP semantics, PUT and DELETE are idempotent, but POST is not idempotent by default because each call usually creates a new server-side effect [[Idempotency#Natural idempotency]].

cases.query: When should an API gateway aggregate responses from multiple backend services instead of keeping that logic in the client or domain services?

expected.heading: Gateway Aggregation
expected.text: The gateway composes a single response from multiple service calls to reduce client round trips.

expected.heading: BFF (Backend for Frontend)
expected.text: Separate gateways or gateway routes per client type (web, mobile, partner API) when each has different payload, latency, or auth requirements.

cases.referenceAnswer: An API gateway should aggregate when you want to reduce client round trips and return one payload tuned for a specific client screen or use case. This is especially useful when different client types need different payload sizes, latency characteristics, or auth requirements, so separate gateway routes or gateways can be justified. The aggregation should stay thin and response-oriented rather than becoming domain logic [[API Gateway#Gateway Aggregation]] [[API Gateway#BFF (Backend for Frontend)]].

cases.query: What edge concerns are appropriate for an API gateway, and what is the main organizational benefit of handling them there?

expected.heading: Gateway Offloading
expected.text: The gateway handles edge concerns such as TLS, compression, CORS, header normalization, and request size limits.

cases.referenceAnswer: An API gateway is a good place for edge concerns like TLS, compression, CORS, header normalization, and request size limits. Centralizing those concerns lets service teams focus on domain behavior, and security and policy changes can roll out in one place [[API Gateway#Gateway Offloading]].

cases.query: How do CP and AP systems behave during a network partition, and what is the key CAP takeaway?

expected.heading: CP behavior (consistency-first during partition)
expected.text: If a partition prevents quorum, writes are rejected or blocked.

expected.heading: AP behavior (availability-first during partition)
expected.text: Replicas accept writes on reachable nodes even when not fully coordinated.

expected.heading: CAP Is About Partition Time, Not Normal Time
expected.text: This is one of the most important interview points: - If links are healthy and quorum is reachable, a CP system can look both consistent and available.

cases.referenceAnswer: During a partition, a CP system prioritizes correctness by requiring leader or quorum confirmation, so writes may be rejected or blocked if quorum is unavailable. An AP system keeps accepting writes on reachable nodes, which preserves service continuity but can produce stale reads or conflict-resolution artifacts.
