expected.text: The Facade pattern provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem.

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: `CheckoutController` orchestrates 5 services directly.

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: `OrderFacade` encapsulates the checkout workflow.

cases.referenceAnswer: A Facade gives clients a simplified interface to a complex subsystem, instead of forcing them to coordinate each service themselves [[Facade#Facade]]. In the checkout example, the controller directly orchestrates five services and knows too much about the workflow [[Facade#Problem]]. The solution is to move that orchestration into `OrderFacade`, so callers use one high-level method rather than manually sequencing the services [[Facade#Solution]].

cases.query: When should you choose Bridge instead of Strategy for payment provider selection?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Use Bridge when you have 2+ implementations today AND expect 2+ abstractions, or vice versa.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: If you only need to swap payment providers (one dimension), Strategy is sufficient: inject `IPaymentGateway` and let the client choose.

cases.referenceAnswer: Strategy is enough when you only need to swap payment providers, because that is a single dimension of variation and the client can choose which strategy to inject [[Bridge#Questions]]. Bridge becomes useful when payment providers and payment types both need to evolve independently, because it separates two dimensions of variation [[Bridge#Questions]]. A practical rule of thumb is to use Bridge when you already have 2+ implementations and expect 2+ abstractions, or vice versa [[Bridge#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: What are the main tradeoffs and pitfalls of using Bridge too early?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: if you only have Stripe today and no concrete plans for PayPal, the Bridge adds two class hierarchies for no current benefit.

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: The break-even is roughly 2×2 = 4 combinations.

cases.referenceAnswer: Bridge can be overkill if you only have one implementation today, because it adds two class hierarchies without immediate benefit [[Bridge#Pitfalls]]. The main costs are extra indirection, harder-to-trace execution, and more classes to maintain [[Bridge#Pitfalls]]. A useful decision rule is to wait until the design is around the 2×2 break-even point, where the number of provider/type combinations makes the extra abstraction worthwhile [[Bridge#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: How do Composite and Visitor work together for an order tree, and when does the tree become a performance problem?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: They're complementary.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: **Treating all components uniformly when some operations only apply to composites** — `IOrderComponent` doesn't expose `Add()`/`Remove()` because leaves don't support children.

cases.referenceAnswer: Composite and Visitor are complementary: Composite defines the tree structure and uniform traversal, while Visitor adds new operations without changing the node classes [[Composite#Questions]]. In the example, visitors like `TaxVisitor`, `ShippingVisitor`, and `DiscountVisitor` can each traverse the same `IOrderComponent` tree [[Composite#Questions]]. The tree becomes a performance problem when it is large, deep, or frequently traversed, because operations like `GetPrice()` walk the entire tree on every call [[Composite#Questions]] [[Composite#Pitfalls]].

cases.query: Why is IEnumerable<string> assignable to IEnumerable<object>, but List<string> is not assignable to List<object>?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- Why does `IEnumerable<string>` assign to `IEnumerable<object>`, but `List<string>` does not assign to `List<object>`? > `IEnumerable<out T>` is covariant, so it is safe to upcast becaus

cases.referenceAnswer: `IEnumerable<string>` can be used where `IEnumerable<object>` is expected because `IEnumerable<out T>` is covariant and only produces values, so upcasting is safe [[Generics#Questions]]. `List<T>` is invariant because it both reads and writes `T`, so treating `List<string>` as `List<object>` would let code insert a non-string object and break type safety [[Generics#Questions]].

cases.query: When should a generic interface type parameter be marked as out or in, and when should it stay invariant?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- Why does `IEnumerable<string>` assign to `IEnumerable<object>`, but `List<string>` does not assign to `List<object>`? > `IEnumerable<out T>` is covariant, so it is safe to upcast becaus

cases.referenceAnswer: Mark a type parameter as `out` when it is output-only and as `in` when it is input-only [[Generics#Questions]]. If the parameter is both consumed and produced, keep it invariant because variance would permit unsafe assignments [[Generics#Questions]].

cases.query: What are the main tradeoffs between generics and inheritance for polymorphism in C#?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Generics express static (compile-time) polymorphism — the type argument is resolved at JIT time.

cases.referenceAnswer: Generics provide static, compile-time polymorphism because the type argument is resolved at JIT time [[Generics#Tradeoffs]]. Inheritance provides dynamic, runtime polymorphism through virtual dispatch [[Generics#Tradeoffs]]. A good rule is to use generics when the concrete type is known at the call site, and inheritance when the concrete type is chosen at runtime, such as for strategies, plugins, or handlers [[Generics#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: Why do generic collections like List<int> avoid boxing compared with non-generic collections such as ArrayList?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: - **Generics vs `object` (boxing)**: Non-generic collections (`ArrayList`, `Hashtable`) store value types as `object`, boxing them on every add and unboxing on every read.

cases.referenceAnswer: Non-generic collections like `ArrayList` store value types as `object`, which means each add boxes the value and each read unboxes it [[Generics#Tradeoffs]]. `List<int>` avoids boxing entirely because the JIT generates a specialized implementation for the value type [[Generics#Tradeoffs]]. This can make a measurable performance difference in allocation-heavy loops over value types such as `int`, `Guid`, or `DateTime` [[Generics#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: What makes a Facade different from a god class, and what should you do if it starts taking on business logic?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: A Facade should be a thin coordinator — it calls subsystems in the right order but doesn't make business decisions.

cases.referenceAnswer: A Facade should stay a thin coordinator that orchestrates subsystems in the right order without making business decisions [[Facade#Questions]]. If it starts calculating discounts, validating business rules, or managing state, it is drifting into a god class anti-pattern [[Facade#Questions]]. The recommended fix is to extract business logic into domain services and keep the facade as a pure orchestrator [[Facade#Questions]].

cases.query: How do common .NET APIs like File, HttpClient, DbContext, and minimal APIs illustrate the Facade pattern?

expected.heading: You Already Use This
expected.text: **`File` static class** — a facade over `FileStream`, `StreamReader`, `StreamWriter`, and `Path`.

cases.referenceAnswer: These APIs are examples of Facade because each one hides a more complex subsystem behind a simpler surface [[Facade#You Already Use This]]. `File` hides stream creation, buffering, encoding, and disposal behind one call [[Facade#You Already Use This]]. `HttpClient`, `DbContext`, and `WebApplication` similarly hide HTTP, database, and hosting machinery behind concise methods [[Facade#You Already Use This]].

cases.query: When should a Facade hide its subsystems, and why is exposing them a problem?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Should a Facade expose the subsystems it wraps, or hide them completely?

cases.referenceAnswer: A Facade should hide the subsystems it wraps rather than expose them directly [[Facade#Questions]]. The note says to hide them completely, because if callers can reach into something like `orderFacade.Payment.ChargeAsync()`, they bypass the facade’s intended orchestration [[Facade#Questions]]. That undermines the purpose of the facade as a single coordinating entry point [[Facade#Questions]].

cases.query: How does the Flyweight pattern reduce memory usage in a product catalog, and what changes are needed to move shared category data out of each Product?

expected.heading: Flyweight
expected.text: The Flyweight pattern reduces memory usage by sharing common state across many fine-grained objects.

expected.heading: Problem
expected.text: Each of 100,000 `Product` instances stores its own copy of category metadata — tax rates, display rules, shipping constraints — even though thousands of products share the same category: ```csharp pub

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: Extract shared category data into a flyweight.

cases.referenceAnswer: The Flyweight pattern reduces memory usage by sharing common state across many fine-grained objects, instead of duplicating it for every instance [[Flyweight]]. In the product example, each Product currently stores its own copy of category metadata even though many products share the same category [[Flyweight#Problem]]. The solution is to extract the shared category data into a flyweight and have products store only a category ID [[Flyweight#Solution]].

cases.query: What is the difference between intrinsic state and extrinsic state in Flyweight, and how does the printing press example illustrate it?

expected.heading: Flyweight
expected.text: A printing press uses shared letter stamps instead of casting a unique metal letter for every character in a book.

cases.referenceAnswer: Intrinsic state is shared and immutable, and it is stored in the flyweight, while extrinsic state is unique per use and passed in by the caller [[Flyweight]]. The printing press example shows this by reusing the same letter stamp many times: the shape of the letter is shared, but its position changes each time [[Flyweight]].

cases.query: What does the Flyweight factory do, and why is it useful when many products share the same category definition?

expected.heading: Flyweight
expected.text: A flyweight factory returns the same instance for objects with identical intrinsic state.

expected.heading: Solution
expected.text: public class CategoryFlyweightFactory {

cases.referenceAnswer: A flyweight factory returns the same instance for objects with identical intrinsic state [[Flyweight]]. In the category example, the factory is used so the same category flyweight can be reused whenever the category is the same [[Flyweight#Solution]]. This avoids storing duplicate category data in every Product and supports the shared-state design described in the problem [[Flyweight#Problem]].

cases.query: When should I use a golden test set versus a targeted eval suite, and why do I need both?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.citationLabel: 11 AI & ML/LLM/Evaluation/Golden Test Set and Regression Runs.md
expected.text: Two complementary types cover the full picture

cases.referenceAnswer: Golden test sets are for broad regression coverage across the system’s normal operating range, while targeted eval suites are for specific failure modes that carry real risk [[Golden Test Set and Regression Runs#Intro]]. The note says these two types are complementary: golden sets catch unexpected regressions across the board, and targeted suites give fast, precise signal on failures that break trust or create harm [[Golden Test Set and Regression Runs#Intro]].
expectedCitations: 11 AI & ML/LLM/Evaluation/Golden Test Set and Regression Runs.md

cases.query: What are the practical signs that an evaluation setup has overfit to the benchmark, and how can you reduce that risk?

expected.heading: Evaluation Overfitting
expected.text: You keep iterating on a prompt until it maximizes a single judge score on your dev set.

cases.referenceAnswer: Evaluation overfitting can show up when you keep optimizing a prompt until it maximizes a single judge score on the dev set, or when improvements appear only on the dev set and not on a holdout set or online metrics [[Evaluation#Evaluation Overfitting]]. The note recommends reducing this risk by introducing multiple evaluation dimensions, adding human spot checks, and keeping a frozen holdout set that you never tune against [[Evaluation#Evaluation Overfitting]].

cases.query: Why should deterministic checks run before an LLM judge, and what kinds of failures do they catch?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: They are cheap (microseconds), deterministic (same input always gives same result), and should run on every evaluation before any LLM judge.

expected.heading: Types of Deterministic Checks
expected.text: | Check type | What it validates | Example | |-----------|------------------|---------| | **Schema validation** | Output is parseable and matches expected structure | JSON schema, required fields, no

cases.referenceAnswer: Deterministic checks should run before an LLM judge because they are cheap, deterministic, and meant to catch obvious failures fast and cheaply before paying for semantic judging [[Deterministic Checks#Intro]]. They validate hard constraints such as schema validity, required fields, safety rules, tool or policy constraints, and other specific checks like PII scanning or length constraints [[Deterministic Checks#Intro]] [[Deterministic Checks#Types of Deterministic Checks]].

cases.query: What is LLM-as-a-judge, and when is it useful compared with absolute scoring or pairwise comparison?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: LLM-as-a-judge is an evaluation pattern where one model grades another model's output against an explicit rubric.

cases.referenceAnswer: LLM-as-a-judge is a pattern where one model grades another model’s output against an explicit rubric, and it is useful for scalable, semantics-aware regression testing when human labels are expensive or slow [[LLM-as-a-Judge#Intro]]. Absolute scoring is best when you need hard pass/fail thresholds, while pairwise comparison is better when quality is subjective or you are iterating quickly and care more about being better than baseline than about a specific number [[LLM-as-a-Judge#Intro]].

cases.query: In ASP.NET Core Web API, how does a request flow from middleware to an endpoint, and where do you typically place logic for validation, auth, and error handling?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: ASP.NET Core Web API runs each HTTP request through a middleware pipeline, then dispatches it to an endpoint

expected.heading: Example
expected.text: Client->>Middleware: Request

expected.heading: Example
expected.text: Minimal API: ```csharp var app = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args).Build();

cases.referenceAnswer: A request enters the middleware pipeline first, and only after routing does ASP.NET Core dispatch it to an endpoint such as a Minimal API handler or a controller action [[ASP.NET Web API#Intro]]. In practice, you decide whether logic belongs in middleware, filters, or endpoint code, especially for validation, input mapping, error handling, and auth [[ASP.NET Web API#Intro]]. The sequence diagram and examples show both the pipeline flow and two endpoint styles: Minimal API and controller-based [[ASP.NET Web API#Example]].

cases.query: What are the tradeoffs between JWT bearer authentication, cookie authentication, and API keys in ASP.NET Core APIs?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: JWT is stateless — the server stores nothing per session, making it ideal for horizontally scaled APIs and mobile/SPA clients.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: For browser-based web applications (Razor Pages/MVC) where the browser manages cookie transmission automatically

expected.heading: References
expected.text: - [Authentication in ASP.NET Core (Microsoft Learn)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/aspnet/core/security/authentication/) — official overview of the authentication middleware, scheme registration,

cases.referenceAnswer: JWT bearer authentication is stateless, which makes it a good fit for horizontally scaled APIs and mobile or SPA clients, but it cannot be revoked before expiry without extra infrastructure [[Authentication#Tradeoffs]][[Authentication#Questions]]. Cookie authentication is simpler for browser-based web apps because the browser handles cookie transmission automatically, and it is easier to revoke [[Authentication#Questions]]. API keys are simpler still and are appropriate for server-to-server authentication where the caller is not an end user [[Authentication#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: When should an ASP.NET Core API use policy-based authorization versus resource-based authorization, and how does that relate to declarative versus imperative checks?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Policy-based is more flexible — requirements are composable, testable, and decouple permission logic from controllers.

expected.heading: References
expected.text: - [Authorization in ASP.NET Core (Microsoft Learn)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/aspnet/core/security/authorization/introduction) — official overview of role-based, claims-based, and policy-based

cases.referenceAnswer: Policy-based authorization is the default choice for most production systems because it is composable, testable, and keeps permission logic out of controllers [[Authorization#Tradeoffs]]. Resource-based authorization is the right fit when the decision depends on the specific resource instance, such as its owner, state, or team membership [[Authorization#Tradeoffs]][[Authorization#References]]. Declarative authorization is cleaner and happens at routing time, while imperative authorization is needed when you only have the resource after loading it from the database [[Authorization#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: What problem do namespaces solve in C#, and what do they not provide compared with assemblies and access modifiers?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A namespace is a logical scope for organizing types (classes, structs, interfaces, enums, delegates) in C#.

cases.referenceAnswer: Namespaces provide a logical scope for organizing types and help prevent naming collisions, making large codebases easier to navigate. They support disambiguation, modularization, and better discoverability in tooling. However, encapsulation and versioning boundaries come from assemblies and access modifiers, not namespaces. [[Namespaces#Intro]]

cases.query: When should a team prefer file-scoped namespaces versus block-scoped namespaces?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Example (file-scoped namespace):

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: File-scoped namespaces reduce indentation and noise for one-namespace-per-file layouts, which improves readability in modern codebases.

cases.referenceAnswer: File-scoped namespaces are a good fit for one-namespace-per-file layouts because they reduce indentation and noise, improving readability. Block-scoped namespaces are still useful when a file intentionally contains multiple namespace blocks or mixed declarations. The note shows both styles as valid C# namespace forms. [[Namespaces#Intro]] [[Namespaces#Tradeoffs]]

cases.query: What are the main benefits of namespaces for organizing a large C# codebase?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: It prevents naming collisions and makes large solutions easier to navigate by grouping related code into a clear, discoverable API surface.

cases.referenceAnswer: Namespaces help organize a large C# codebase by preventing naming collisions and grouping related code into a clear, discoverable API surface. They also provide disambiguation for type names, logical modularization, and better readability and discoverability in tooling. In short, they make large solutions easier to navigate. [[Namespaces#Intro]]

cases.query: What are the common namespace-related pitfalls described in the note, and how can they be avoided?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: when two NuGet packages define the same fully qualified type name, the compiler reports ambiguity.

cases.referenceAnswer: One pitfall is namespace collision between libraries, where two NuGet packages define the same fully qualified type name and the compiler reports ambiguity. The note says this can be fixed with extern alias in the project file, or by completing the migration before removing the old package. Another pitfall is global using overreach, since global using directives apply to every file in the project; the recommended mitigation is to limit them to universally unambiguous imports and keep domain-specific namespaces in per-file using directives. [[Namespaces#Pitfalls]]

cases.query: When should a team choose a modular monolith instead of microservices, and what tradeoffs does that choice avoid?

expected.heading: Microservices vs monolith vs modular monolith
expected.text: | Dimension | Monolith | Modular Monolith | Microservices | |---|---|---|---| | Deployments | Single unit | Single unit with strict module boundaries | Independent service deployments | | Team model |

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A modular monolith is a single deployable application that is intentionally split into strict modules with explicit boundaries.

expected.heading: Mechanism
expected.text: Each module owns its own domain model, use cases, persistence rules, and public contract.

cases.referenceAnswer: A modular monolith is a good choice when the product is growing, domain boundaries are becoming clear, and the team wants to avoid the operational overhead of many services. It preserves many of the benefits people want from microservices, such as clear ownership and clean contracts, while keeping a single deployable unit. By contrast, microservices are a better fit for large organizations with high release velocity and independent scaling needs. [[Microservices#Microservices vs monolith vs modular monolith]] [[Modular Monolith#Intro]] [[Modular Monolith#Mechanism]]

cases.query: What problems can happen in event-driven systems with duplicate or out-of-order messages, and how should consumers handle them?

expected.heading: 1) Event Ordering
expected.text: distributed brokers and parallel consumers do not guarantee global ordering.

expected.heading: 2) Idempotency
expected.text: duplicate delivery causes duplicate side effects (double charge, duplicate email, repeated inventory decrement).

expected.heading: Consumer (Billing Service)
expected.text: Idempotency key: message.EventId (store processed IDs in durable storage).

cases.referenceAnswer: Event-driven systems can process messages out of order because distributed brokers and parallel consumers do not guarantee global ordering. They can also deliver the same message more than once, which can cause duplicate side effects like double charges or repeated inventory changes. To handle this, consumers should use per-aggregate ordering when needed, include version or sequence information, detect stale events, and make handlers idempotent with deterministic keys such as EventId. [[Event-Driven Architecture#1) Event Ordering]] [[Event-Driven Architecture#2) Idempotency]] [[Event-Driven Architecture#Consumer (Billing Service)]]

cases.query: How should a modular monolith be structured so that modules stay isolated but can still collaborate, and what .NET dependency rule illustrates this?

expected.heading: Mechanism
expected.text: Each module owns its own domain model, use cases, persistence rules, and public contract.

expected.heading: .NET Implementation
expected.text: ```text src/ Modules/ Orders/ Orders.Contracts/ Orders.Core/ Orders.Infrastructure/ Inventory/ Inventory.Contracts/ Inventory.Core/ Inventory.Infrastructure/ Host/ Shared.Kernel/ ``` `Orders.Core` dep

cases.referenceAnswer: A modular monolith should give each module ownership of its domain model, use cases, persistence rules, and public contract. Modules should communicate only through contracts such as interfaces, commands, events, and DTOs, not by referencing each other’s internal classes. In .NET, one example is Orders.Core depending on Inventory.Contracts rather than Inventory.Core, which keeps the boundary clean while still allowing collaboration in process. [[Modular Monolith#Mechanism]] [[Modular Monolith#.NET Implementation]]

cases.query: What are the main tradeoffs between a monolith, modular monolith, and microservices?

expected.heading: Microservices vs monolith vs modular monolith
expected.text: Single unit with strict module boundaries

cases.referenceAnswer: A monolith is a single unit, a modular monolith is still a single unit but with strict module boundaries, and microservices are independently deployed services. The operational complexity rises across that spectrum from low to low-to-medium to high. Microservices are best when a large organization needs high release velocity and independent scaling, while a modular monolith is a pragmatic middle ground for growing teams. [[Microservices#Microservices vs monolith vs modular monolith]] [[Modular Monolith#Intro]]

cases.query: How do event-driven systems differ from functional programming in the way they model work, and what benefits do they each emphasize?

expected.heading: Event-Driven Development
expected.text: Event-driven development builds systems around *events* — immutable facts that something happened — and reactions to those events.

expected.heading: Functional Programming
expected.text: Functional programming (FP) is a paradigm that models computation as the evaluation of pure functions over immutable data.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Programming paradigms are different ways to model problems: object-oriented, functional, procedural, and event-driven styles.

cases.referenceAnswer: Event-driven development models work as events and reactions to those events, while functional programming models computation as pure functions over immutable data. Event-driven systems emphasize decoupling producers from consumers, and FP emphasizes reasoning, testing, and parallelization through purity and immutability. Both are described as programming paradigms, but they solve problems with different mental models [[Event-driven#Event-Driven Development]] [[Functional Programming]] [[Paradigms#Intro]]

cases.query: Why is the Outbox pattern needed for distributed events, and how does it relate to the event-driven model?

expected.heading: Event-Driven Development
expected.text: Producers publish events without knowing who will consume them; consumers subscribe and handle events independently.

expected.heading: Distributed Events and the Outbox Pattern
expected.text: Publishing to a message broker after a database write introduces a reliability gap: the DB write succeeds but the broker publish fails, leaving the event lost.

cases.referenceAnswer: Event-driven systems let producers publish events without knowing who will consume them, but distributed publishing can fail after the database write succeeds. The Outbox pattern addresses that reliability gap by storing the event in the same transaction as the domain change, then having a background worker publish it later and retry until acknowledged. This preserves the decoupled event-driven model while making delivery more reliable [[Event-driven#Event-Driven Development]] [[Event-driven#Distributed Events and the Outbox Pattern]]

cases.query: What do integration tests validate that unit tests may miss, and how can WebApplicationFactory be used to set them up in ASP.NET Core?

expected.heading: Integration Testing
expected.text: An integration test verifies that multiple components work correctly together — including real infrastructure like databases, HTTP clients, message queues, and configuration.

expected.heading: ASP.NET Core Integration Testing with `WebApplicationFactory`
expected.text: The standard approach in .NET is `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Testing.WebApplicationFactory<T>`, which boots the real application in-process with a test HTTP client: ```csharp public class OrdersApiTests

cases.referenceAnswer: Integration tests verify that multiple components work correctly together, including real infrastructure such as databases, HTTP clients, message queues, and configuration. They can catch issues that unit tests often miss, such as wrong SQL queries, misconfigured DI registrations, broken serialization contracts, and middleware ordering problems. In ASP.NET Core, a standard setup is WebApplicationFactory<T>, which boots the real application in-process and lets you customize services for testing [[Integration Testing]] [[Integration Testing#ASP.NET Core Integration Testing with WebApplicationFactory]]

cases.query: Why do immutable data and pure functions make code easier to test and reason about?

expected.heading: Pure Functions
expected.text: A pure function has no side effects and is referentially transparent — you can replace a call with its return value without changing program behavior.

expected.heading: Immutability
expected.text: Immutable data cannot be changed after creation.

expected.heading: Functional Programming
expected.text: The payoff: code that is easier to reason about, test in isolation, and parallelize — because a pure function with the same inputs always produces the same output and never touches shared state.

cases.referenceAnswer: Pure functions are easier to reason about because they have no side effects and are referentially transparent, so a call can be replaced with its return value without changing behavior. Immutable data cannot be changed after creation, which avoids bugs from shared mutable state. Together, these properties make code easier to test in isolation and safer to run in parallel [[Functional Programming#Pure Functions]] [[Functional Programming#Immutability]] [[Functional Programming]]

cases.query: What is the difference between authentication and authorization in ASP.NET Core, and what authentication schemes are commonly used for APIs versus web apps?

expected.heading: Authentication in ASP.NET Core
expected.text: Authentication is the process of verifying *who* a caller is.

expected.heading: Cookie Authentication
expected.text: For web applications (Razor Pages, MVC), cookie authentication stores the identity in an encrypted cookie:

cases.referenceAnswer: Authentication verifies who the caller is, while authorization determines what the caller can do and happens after authentication [[Authentication#Authentication in ASP.NET Core]]. For APIs, the common schemes mentioned are JWT Bearer tokens and API Keys [[Authentication#Authentication in ASP.NET Core]]. For web applications like Razor Pages and MVC, cookie authentication stores the identity in an encrypted cookie [[Authentication#Cookie Authentication]].

cases.query: How does JWT Bearer authentication work in ASP.NET Core, and what does the server validate?

expected.heading: Authentication in ASP.NET Core
expected.text: Authentication is the process of verifying *who* a caller is.

expected.heading: JWT Bearer Authentication
expected.text: JWT (JSON Web Token) is the standard for stateless API authentication.

cases.referenceAnswer: In ASP.NET Core, authentication is the process of verifying who a caller is, and JWT Bearer is presented as the standard for stateless API authentication [[Authentication#Authentication in ASP.NET Core]] [[Authentication#JWT Bearer Authentication]]. The client sends a signed token in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header [[Authentication#JWT Bearer Authentication]]. The server then validates the token’s signature and expiry without needing a database lookup [[Authentication#JWT Bearer Authentication]].

cases.query: When is blockchain justified, and why is it a poor fit for many internal systems?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A blockchain is an append-only ledger where records are grouped into blocks and linked together using cryptographic hashes.

expected.heading: Using Blockchain When a Database Suffices
expected.text: teams adopt blockchain for internal systems where all parties trust a central authority, gaining none of the decentralization benefits while paying the full cost in throughput, complexity, and complia

cases.referenceAnswer: A blockchain is an append-only ledger linked by cryptographic hashes, but it comes with a fundamental throughput cost from decentralized consensus [[Block-chain#Intro]]. It is a poor fit for internal systems where all parties already trust a central authority, because you pay the cost in throughput, complexity, and compliance risk without getting decentralization benefits [[Block-chain#Using Blockchain When a Database Suffices]]. Blockchain is justified only when you need a shared ledger across mutually distrusting parties with no central authority [[Block-chain#Using Blockchain When a Database Suffices]].

cases.query: Why does changing a past block in a blockchain break the chain?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The hash link makes tampering detectable: changing a past block changes its hash, which breaks the chain unless all subsequent blocks are recomputed

expected.heading: Example
expected.text: Toy example: each block commits to the previous block hash.

cases.referenceAnswer: Each block commits to the previous block hash, so changing an earlier block changes its hash and breaks the chain unless all later blocks are recomputed [[Block-chain#Intro]] [[Block-chain#Example]]. This is what makes tampering detectable in a blockchain [[Block-chain#Intro]].

cases.query: What is the relationship between DevOps, CI/CD pipelines, and deployment strategies when shipping changes safely to production?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: DevOps is about shortening the path from change to production safely: automation, observability, and ownership of runtime behavior.

expected.heading: Deployment Strategies
expected.text: Deployment strategies control how new versions of software reach production.

expected.heading: CI/CD Tools
expected.text: CI/CD pipelines automate the path from code commit to production deployment.

cases.referenceAnswer: DevOps focuses on shortening the path from change to production safely through automation, observability, and ownership of runtime behavior [[09 DevOps#Intro]]. CI/CD pipelines automate the path from code commit to production deployment, while deployment strategies control how new versions reach production [[CI CD tools#CI/CD Tools]] [[Deployment Strategies#Deployment Strategies]]. Together, they support predictable delivery and fast recovery when things break [[09 DevOps#Intro]].

cases.query: When would an all-at-once deployment be appropriate, and what are its main risks?

expected.heading: Deployment Strategies
expected.text: The right strategy balances risk tolerance, infrastructure cost, and rollback speed.

expected.heading: All-at-Once (Big Bang)
expected.text: Downtime is acceptable; infrastructure cost is the constraint.

cases.referenceAnswer: An all-at-once deployment is appropriate when downtime is acceptable and infrastructure cost matters more than availability, such as internal tools, dev/staging environments, or services with contractually acceptable brief downtime [[Deployment Strategies#Deployment Strategies]] [[Deployment Strategies#All-at-Once (Big Bang)]]. Its main risks are full downtime during the swap, immediate impact to all users if the new version is broken, and rollback requiring another full deploy cycle [[Deployment Strategies#All-at-Once (Big Bang)]].

cases.query: How does a rolling deployment work, and what operational tradeoffs does it introduce?

expected.heading: Deployment Strategies
expected.text: A strategy is only as good as your monitoring — without metrics and alerts, you cannot detect a bad deploy fast enough to stop it.

expected.heading: In-Place (Rolling)
expected.text: Replace instances one at a time (or in small batches), keeping the rest serving traffic.

cases.referenceAnswer: A rolling deployment replaces instances one at a time or in small batches while the rest continue serving traffic, so users typically see no downtime [[Deployment Strategies#In-Place (Rolling)]]. The tradeoff is that old and new versions run simultaneously, so the API must remain backward-compatible and rollback is slower because it requires another rolling update in reverse [[Deployment Strategies#In-Place (Rolling)]]. Good monitoring is important because without metrics and alerts, a bad deploy may not be detected quickly enough to stop it [[Deployment Strategies#Deployment Strategies]].

cases.query: What do CI and CD mean in a pipeline, and which .NET CI/CD tools are highlighted as common options?

expected.heading: CI/CD Tools
expected.text: CI (Continuous Integration) runs builds and tests on every commit to catch regressions early.

cases.referenceAnswer: CI means Continuous Integration, where builds and tests run on every commit to catch regressions early [[CI CD tools#CI/CD Tools]]. CD means Continuous Delivery or Deployment, where the release process is automated so every passing build can be deployed with minimal manual intervention [[CI CD tools#CI/CD Tools]]. The note highlights GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps Pipelines, and Jenkins as the three dominant tools for .NET teams [[CI CD tools#CI/CD Tools]].

cases.query: How do records compare to regular classes for data-centric models, and what compiler-generated members do positional records get?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Records are C# types (introduced in C# 9 for classes, C# 10 for structs) designed for data-centric models.

expected.heading: What the Compiler Generates
expected.text: For a positional record: ```csharp public record Person(string Name, int Age); ``` The compiler produces: - A primary constructor with `Name` and `Age` parameters.

expected.heading: Positional vs Nominal Syntax
expected.text: ```csharp // Positional — primary constructor, auto-generated properties and Deconstruct public record Order(int Id, string Customer, decimal Total); // Nominal — explicit properties, no Deconstruct u

cases.referenceAnswer: Records are designed for data-centric models, and they reduce boilerplate for types whose identity is based on content rather than reference [[Records#Intro]]. For a positional record, the compiler generates a primary constructor, init-only properties, value-based equality, `ToString`, `Deconstruct`, and `with` support [[Records#What the Compiler Generates]]. Positional and nominal records both get the same equality, `ToString`, and `with` support, but positional syntax is better for simple data carriers [[Records#Positional vs Nominal Syntax]].

cases.query: When should I choose positional record syntax versus nominal record syntax, and what differences do they have in generated behavior?

expected.heading: What the Compiler Generates
expected.text: Adding extra properties or methods is free — the generated members incorporate them.

expected.heading: Positional vs Nominal Syntax
expected.text: Use positional for simple data carriers; nominal when you need validation, computed properties, or custom accessors.

cases.referenceAnswer: Use positional record syntax when you want a simple data carrier with automatically generated properties and `Deconstruct` support [[Records#Positional vs Nominal Syntax]]. Use nominal record syntax when you need validation, computed properties, or custom accessors [[Records#Positional vs Nominal Syntax]]. In both forms, the record still gets the same equality, `ToString`, and `with` support, and extra properties or methods are incorporated into the generated members [[Records#What the Compiler Generates]] [[Records#Positional vs Nominal Syntax]].

cases.query: What exactly does the compiler generate for a positional record, and can those generated members be customized?

expected.heading: What the Compiler Generates
expected.text: You can override any of these.

cases.referenceAnswer: For a positional record, the compiler generates a primary constructor, properties, value-based equality members, `GetHashCode`, `==` and `!=`, `ToString`, `Deconstruct`, a copy constructor and `<Clone>$` for `with` expressions, and an `EqualityContract` property for record classes [[Records#What the Compiler Generates]]. It also generates `init`-only properties for `record class` and `get`/`set` properties for `record struct` [[Records#What the Compiler Generates]]. Any of these generated members can be overridden [[Records#What the Compiler Generates]].

cases.query: What is observability, and why should teams add it from day one instead of retrofitting it later?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Observability is the ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs: metrics, logs, and traces.

cases.referenceAnswer: Observability is the ability to understand a system’s internal state from its external outputs, specifically metrics, logs, and traces [[Observability#Intro]]. In distributed systems, it helps move from symptoms to causes quickly because failures often cross service boundaries and do not appear as one obvious exception [[Observability#Intro]]. Teams should add it from day one, since retrofitting it later is harder when the needed telemetry was never emitted [[Observability#Intro]].

cases.query: How do metrics, logs, and traces differ, and what kinds of questions do metrics and logs answer?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Observability is the ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs: metrics, logs, and traces.

expected.heading: Metrics
expected.text: Metrics are numeric measurements over time that answer "how much" and "how often".

expected.heading: Logs
expected.text: Logs are structured event records that answer "what exactly happened" at a point in time.

cases.referenceAnswer: Metrics, logs, and traces are the external outputs used to understand a system’s internal state [[Observability#Intro]]. Metrics are numeric measurements over time that answer “how much” and “how often” [[Observability#Metrics]]. Logs are structured event records that answer “what exactly happened” at a point in time [[Observability#Logs]].

cases.query: What metric types and service-health methods should be used to monitor a service, and what do they measure?

expected.heading: Metrics
expected.text: Metrics are numeric measurements over time that answer "how much" and "how often".

cases.referenceAnswer: Metrics are numeric measurements over time that answer “how much” and “how often” [[Observability#Metrics]]. For service-level health, the RED method focuses on rate, errors, and duration, where duration should be treated as a distribution rather than just an average [[Observability#Metrics]]. For resource-level health, the USE method focuses on utilization, saturation, and errors [[Observability#Metrics]]. Histograms are useful for latency because they let the backend estimate percentiles like p50, p95, and p99 [[Observability#Metrics]].

cases.query: What are the main deployment strategies described, and how do blue-green, canary, and linear differ in traffic shifting and rollback behavior?

expected.heading: Blue-Green
expected.text: Maintain two identical environments (blue = current, green = new).

expected.heading: Canary
expected.text: Route a small percentage of real traffic to the new version, monitor, then gradually increase.

expected.heading: Linear
expected.text: Increase traffic to the new version in fixed increments on a fixed schedule (e.g., +10% every 10 minutes).

cases.referenceAnswer: Blue-green deployment keeps two identical environments and switches traffic atomically by updating the load balancer [[Deployment Strategies#Blue-Green]]. Canary deployment sends a small percentage of real traffic to the new version, monitors it, and increases traffic gradually if it stays healthy [[Deployment Strategies#Canary]]. Linear deployment also increases traffic gradually, but it does so on a fixed schedule rather than based on metrics, and it is described as time-driven rather than metric-driven [[Deployment Strategies#Linear]].

cases.query: What preprocessing steps should I consider to make raw training data usable while avoiding leakage, and how should I split the data to estimate generalization correctly?

expected.heading: Data Cleaning and Preprocessing
expected.text: Make the raw data usable: handle missing values, outliers, duplicates, schema drift, and text normalization, while keeping transformations deterministic.

expected.heading: Train Test Validation Split
expected.text: Split data to simulate the future: random splits work for IID data, but time based or group based splits are safer for temporal, user, or session correlated data.

cases.referenceAnswer: You should clean the data by handling missing values, outliers, duplicates, schema drift, and text normalization, while keeping transformations deterministic. For evaluation, split the data in a way that simulates the future: random splits are fine for IID data, but time-based or group-based splits are safer when observations are temporally or otherwise correlated. Also keep a true holdout test set untouched until the end to estimate generalization. [[Machine Learning#Data Cleaning and Preprocessing]] [[Machine Learning#Train Test Validation Split]]

cases.query: How do I decide between simple and complex features, and what methods should I use to select features when multiple teams share them?

expected.heading: Feature Engineering and Selection
expected.text: Turn raw columns into signals the model can learn, such as aggregates, time windows, text features, or embeddings, depending on the problem type ([[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/Machine Learning/Typ

cases.referenceAnswer: Feature engineering turns raw columns into signals the model can learn, such as aggregates, time windows, text features, or embeddings, depending on the problem. When choosing features, balance simple and stable features against more complex ones that may improve accuracy but increase operational risk. For selection, start with domain constraints, then use model-based importance and ablation tests, and consider a feature store if many teams share features. [[Machine Learning#Feature Engineering and Selection]]

cases.query: What is data drift, and why can a model degrade silently after deployment even if nothing obvious breaks?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Data drift is when the statistical properties of your input data change over time compared to the data your model was trained on.

cases.referenceAnswer: Data drift happens when the statistical properties of input data change over time compared with the data a model was trained on. This matters because models assume training and serving data come from the same distribution, so predictions can become less reliable even when there is no obvious error. In practice, a model may silently degrade as the environment changes. [[Data Drift#Intro]]

cases.query: How can I compose small functions in C# to validate and normalize a string input before checking it?

expected.heading: Function Composition
expected.text: Building complex behavior by chaining small, single-purpose functions.

cases.referenceAnswer: You can build the validation as a pipeline where one function normalizes the input and another checks whether it is valid. In the example, `normalize` trims and lowercases the string, and `isValidInput` applies `isValid(normalize(s))` so the checks run on the cleaned value. This shows composition as wiring small functions together into one behavior [[Functional Programming#Function Composition]].

cases.query: Why does the functional programming example use `Where`, `Select`, and `Sum` instead of a loop, and what result does it produce?

expected.heading: Higher-Order Functions
expected.text: Functions that take other functions as arguments or return functions.

cases.referenceAnswer: The example uses higher-order functions because `Where`, `Select`, and `Sum` each accept or return functions as part of a pipeline. The sequence filters orders above 60, applies a 10% markup, and then sums the results without mutation or a loop variable. The comment shows the final total is 330 [[Functional Programming#Higher-Order Functions]].

cases.query: When should I use in-process events versus a distributed broker, and what reliability pattern should I pair with distributed events?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: start with in-process events for domain logic within a single service.

expected.heading: Publishing Before Persisting
expected.text: **What goes wrong**: the event is published to the broker before the database transaction commits.

cases.referenceAnswer: Start with in-process events when the logic stays within a single service or bounded context, because they are simple and require no extra infrastructure. Move to a distributed broker when you need cross-service communication, durability across restarts, or fan-out to multiple consumers. For distributed events, pair them with the Outbox pattern and publish only after a successful commit to avoid losing or misordering events [[Event-driven#Tradeoffs]] [[Event-driven#Publishing Before Persisting]].

cases.query: How should I test dependencies like databases, HTTP services, message queues, and clocks in integration tests?

expected.heading: When to Use Real Infrastructure vs In-Memory Substitutes
expected.text: | Dependency | Test approach | Reason | |---|---|---| | SQL database | Testcontainers (real Docker DB) or EF In-Memory | In-memory misses SQL-specific behavior (constraints, transactions, indexes) | |

cases.referenceAnswer: For SQL databases, use Testcontainers or EF In-Memory, but prefer real containers when you need real database behavior such as constraints and transactions. For HTTP services, use WireMock.Net or an `HttpMessageHandler` fake to avoid real network calls and to test error scenarios. For message queues, use an in-memory fake or Testcontainers, and for clocks use `FakeTimeProvider` so time-sensitive tests are deterministic [[Integration Testing#When to Use Real Infrastructure vs In-Memory Substitutes]].

cases.query: When should I use RAG instead of prompting or classic ML for an NLP application, and why?

expected.heading: Map of the Section
expected.text: - **[[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning|Machine Learning]]** — the classic discipline: training pipelines, [[Software Engineering/11 AI & ML/Machine Learning/Types/Type

expected.heading: Choosing an Approach
expected.text: The first engineering decision is usually not which model — it is which class of solution

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Evaluation is how you measure whether an LLM application is doing the right thing

cases.referenceAnswer: Use classic ML when you have labeled data, an explicit prediction target, and constraints like latency or cost, because it offers millisecond inference and well-understood evaluation [[11 AI & ML#Choosing an Approach]]. Use LLM prompting when the task is open-ended language work with no training data and you want fast iteration [[11 AI & ML#Choosing an Approach]]. Choose RAG when you need an LLM plus current or private knowledge, since it updates knowledge without retraining and keeps answers traceable to sources [[11 AI & ML#Choosing an Approach]]. Whatever you choose, evaluation should cover answer quality, grounding, safety, and regressions over time [[Evaluation#Intro]].

cases.query: How do transformers changed NLP, and what production options exist for doing sentiment analysis in .NET?

expected.heading: The Transformer Impact
expected.text: Before transformers (2017), NLP tasks required separate models for each task (LSTM for translation, CNN for classification, CRF for NER).

expected.heading: NLP in .NET
expected.text: For production NLP in .NET, the primary options are:

expected.heading: Sentiment Analysis
expected.text: Classifying the emotional tone of text: positive, negative, neutral

cases.referenceAnswer: Transformers changed NLP by enabling transfer learning: a large model is pre-trained on massive text corpora and then fine-tuned on a smaller task-specific dataset [[Natural Language Processing#The Transformer Impact]]. In .NET, production NLP options include Azure AI Language for cloud-based sentiment analysis, ML.NET for on-device sentiment analysis, and Microsoft Agent Framework or Azure OpenAI for LLM-based tasks [[Natural Language Processing#NLP in .NET]]. Sentiment analysis itself classifies the emotional tone of text, and it can also be done at the aspect level rather than only for the whole text [[Natural Language Processing#Sentiment Analysis]].

cases.query: What embedding model and similarity setup would be a good practical choice for a RAG system, and how can dimensionality affect it?

expected.heading: Model Families
expected.text: Three categories dominate production RAG systems:

expected.heading: Dimensionality
expected.text: Higher dimensions give the model more room to represent fine-grained distinctions.

expected.heading: Similarity Metrics
expected.text: For most RAG systems: use cosine similarity unless the model documentation specifically

cases.referenceAnswer: For production RAG systems, a common choice is a proprietary API embedding model such as OpenAI text-embedding-3-small or text-embedding-3-large, or a local Sentence Transformers model if you want to avoid per-token cost [[Embeddings#Model Families]]. Higher dimensions can improve representational detail, but they also increase storage, compute, and ANN index memory, so a practical pattern is to index at reduced dimensions and re-rank with full-dimensional vectors when needed [[Embeddings#Dimensionality]]. For similarity, cosine similarity is the default choice for most embedding models, unless the model documentation recommends something else [[Embeddings#Similarity Metrics]].

cases.query: Why is evaluation especially important for LLM applications compared with deterministic software?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Because LLM output is probabilistic and open-ended

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The model is rarely the hard part.

cases.referenceAnswer: Evaluation matters because LLM output is probabilistic and open-ended, so you cannot depend on a single pass/fail assertion the way you would for deterministic code [[Evaluation#Intro]]. Instead, evaluation needs to combine hard checks, semantic judges, regression sets, and production signals [[Evaluation#Intro]]. More broadly, the engineering challenge is often not the model itself but the surrounding data quality, evaluation discipline, guardrails, and monitoring [[11 AI & ML#Intro]].

cases.query: How do record classes compare to regular classes when choosing between identity semantics and value equality, and what does with-expressions behavior imply for records?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Regular class (manual equality, mutable by default)

expected.heading: with-expressions
expected.text: Non-destructive mutation — creates a new instance with selected properties changed:

cases.referenceAnswer: Use a regular class when you want identity semantics, manual equality, and mutable-by-default behavior, and use a record class when value equality and immutability by convention are a better fit [[Classes#Tradeoffs]]. Records also support non-destructive mutation with `with`, which creates a new instance with selected properties changed rather than modifying the original [[Records#with-expressions]].

cases.query: What are the main tradeoffs between abstract classes and interfaces, and which modifier combinations are allowed for partial classes?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: Abstract class (shared state + implementation, single inheritance)

expected.heading: Modifier Compatibility
expected.text: `partial` + any modifier | Yes

cases.referenceAnswer: An abstract class is the better choice when you need shared state, constructors, or a template-method style base with implementation, while an interface is better when you need multiple implementations or only a contract with no instance state [[Classes#Tradeoffs]]. For partial classes, `partial` can be combined with any modifier, including `abstract` and `sealed` [[Classes#Modifier Compatibility]].

cases.query: How do you avoid multicast delegate or event handlers stopping after one subscriber throws, and what is the recommended pattern for preventing event leaks?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Multicast stops at first exception

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: subscribers stay alive while subscribed.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Prevent with explicit unsubscribe (`Dispose`), weak-event pattern, or scoped subscription helpers.

cases.referenceAnswer: A multicast delegate or event invocation stops at the first exception, so the safe pattern is to iterate `GetInvocationList()` and invoke each handler in its own `try/catch` [[Delegates#Pitfalls]]. Event leaks happen because the publisher keeps strong references to subscriber handlers, so subscribers can stay alive while subscribed [[Events#Pitfalls]]. To prevent that, unsubscribe explicitly, typically via `Dispose`, or use a weak-event or scoped subscription pattern [[Events#Questions]].

cases.query: When should you use events instead of public delegate fields or IObservable<T>, and what access-control advantage do events provide?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: A public delegate field lets any external caller replace, null out, or directly invoke the handler.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: An event exposes only `add`/`remove` from outside the declaring type.

cases.referenceAnswer: Use an event rather than a public delegate field when you want to preserve publisher control, because external callers can only subscribe or unsubscribe and cannot invoke, replace, or null out the handler [[Events#Tradeoffs]]. Choose `IObservable<T>` when you need stream operators such as filtering, merging, debouncing, retrying, or async continuations; use events for simple synchronous notifications [[Events#Tradeoffs]].

cases.query: How do structs differ from classes in C# in terms of value vs reference semantics, inheritance, and when to use each?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A struct is a value type in C#.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A class is a reference type that defines a blueprint for objects allocated on the managed heap.

cases.referenceAnswer: Structs are value types, so assignment copies the value inline, while classes are reference types, so multiple variables can refer to the same object and see shared mutations [[Structs#Intro]] [[Classes#Intro]]. Structs are always sealed and cannot participate in class inheritance, whereas classes support single-class inheritance [[Structs#Intro]] [[Classes#Intro]]. The notes suggest using structs for small, immutable data and classes for services, domain entities, and infrastructure types [[Structs#Intro]] [[Classes#Intro]].

cases.query: What are the rules and benefits of using readonly struct in C#?

expected.heading: readonly struct
expected.text: Fields must be `readonly`, and properties must be get-only or `init`-only.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A struct is a value type in C#.

cases.referenceAnswer: A readonly struct requires its fields to be readonly and its properties to be get-only or init-only, and the compiler enforces immutability after construction [[Structs#readonly struct]]. This can reduce defensive copies when the struct is accessed through in parameters or readonly fields [[Structs#readonly struct]]. The broader guidance is that structs are a good fit for small, immutable data representing one logical value [[Structs#Intro]].

cases.query: How do delegates and multicast delegates behave in C#?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A delegate is a type-safe function pointer in C#.

expected.heading: Multicast Delegates
expected.text: Delegates can hold an invocation list (`+=`, `-=`).

cases.referenceAnswer: A delegate is a type-safe function pointer that lets you treat methods as values, store them, pass them around, and invoke them later [[Delegates#Intro]]. Delegates can also hold an invocation list using += and -=, and calling the delegate invokes handlers in registration order [[Delegates#Multicast Delegates]]. If one handler throws, later handlers are not invoked, and for non-void delegates only the last handler's return value is returned [[Delegates#Multicast Delegates]].

cases.query: What built-in generic delegate types are commonly used in C# and how do they differ?

expected.heading: Built-in generic delegates
expected.text: - `Action<T...>`: returns `void` - `Func<T..., TResult>`: returns a value - `Predicate<T>`: predefined delegate with signature `T -> bool` (similar to `Func<T, bool>`, but a different type) ```csharp

cases.referenceAnswer: Action<T...> is used for delegates that return void, while Func<T..., TResult> is used for delegates that return a value [[Delegates#Built-in generic delegates]]. Predicate<T> is a predefined delegate with signature T -> bool, similar to Func<T, bool> but it is a different type [[Delegates#Built-in generic delegates]].

cases.query: How can a modular monolith be extracted into microservices without rewriting the whole system?

expected.heading: Extraction Path to Microservices
expected.text: If boundaries are real, extraction is mechanical instead of a rewrite.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Microservices are an architecture style where a system is split into independently deployable services, each aligned to a business capability and owning its own data.

cases.referenceAnswer: A modular monolith can be extracted incrementally if the boundaries are real: keep call sites targeting contracts, swap the in-process implementation for an HTTP or gRPC client behind the same interface, and then move the module runtime into its own deployable service with owned data [[Modular Monolith#Extraction Path to Microservices]]. Microservices are the destination architecture when you want independently deployable services that own their own data [[Microservices#Intro]]. The key idea is that the contract stays stable while the implementation and deployment change [[Modular Monolith#Extraction Path to Microservices]].

cases.query: What problems happen when module boundaries erode or modules share a database, and how can that be prevented?

expected.heading: 1 Boundary erosion through shortcuts
expected.text: developers start reading other modules tables directly or referencing internals for speed, and modules collapse into hidden coupling.

expected.heading: 2 Shared database without isolation
expected.text: modules become coupled through shared tables, migration order dependencies, and cross module joins.

cases.referenceAnswer: Boundary erosion happens when developers take shortcuts like reading other modules’ tables directly or referencing internals, which creates hidden coupling [[Modular Monolith#1 Boundary erosion through shortcuts]]. Shared databases create a similar problem because modules become coupled through shared tables, migration order dependencies, and cross-module joins [[Modular Monolith#2 Shared database without isolation]]. The suggested defenses are to enforce contracts, block forbidden references with architecture tests, give each module its own schema and DbContext, and treat cross-module data needs as API or event-driven integration [[Modular Monolith#1 Boundary erosion through shortcuts]] [[Modular Monolith#2 Shared database without isolation]].

cases.query: When should a team choose a modular monolith instead of microservices for a new product?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Clear domains with limited platform capacity often fit modular monolith.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: You usually reach for microservices when team count grows, deployment independence becomes a bottleneck, and domains have different scaling or availability needs.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: This is not inherently bad: fewer moving parts, simpler operations, easy local development, and no distributed systems complexity.

cases.referenceAnswer: A modular monolith is a good fit when the domains are clear but platform capacity is limited, because it preserves structure without immediately taking on distributed-systems overhead [[Microservices#Questions]] [[Monolith Architecture#Intro]]. Microservices are more appropriate when team count grows, deployment independence becomes a bottleneck, or different domains need different scaling or availability characteristics [[Microservices#Intro]]. In other words, start with the simpler operational model unless the organization’s release and scaling needs justify the extra complexity [[Monolith Architecture#Intro]] [[Microservices#Intro]].

cases.query: What signs show that a monolith has turned into a big ball of mud, and how does a modular monolith address those problems without moving to microservices?

expected.heading: When Monoliths Break Down
expected.text: | Signal | What it means | |--------|--------------| | Build takes 10+ minutes | Too much code in one compilation unit | | Deploying one feature requires testing everything | Hidden coupling between c

expected.heading: Modular Monolith — The Middle Ground
expected.text: A modular monolith enforces explicit module boundaries within a single deployment

expected.heading: Monolith vs Microservices
expected.text: | Deployment | Single unit | Independent per service |

cases.referenceAnswer: A monolith is showing trouble when build times get long, feature changes require testing everything, teams break each other’s work, scaling is coupled, or schema changes require coordination across teams; those are signs the system has become a big ball of mud because module boundaries were not enforced [[Monolith Architecture#When Monoliths Break Down]]. A modular monolith keeps a single deployment but enforces explicit module boundaries, with modules communicating through public interfaces instead of reaching into each other’s internals [[Monolith Architecture#Modular Monolith — The Middle Ground]]. Compared with microservices, it preserves the monolith’s single-unit deployment while gaining some of the maintainability benefits of service-style boundaries [[Monolith Architecture#Monolith vs Microservices]].

cases.query: When should a system use synchronous calls versus asynchronous messaging, and what operational tradeoffs come with each choice?

expected.heading: Communication patterns
expected.text: Use synchronous communication when the caller needs an immediate answer.

expected.heading: 4) Distributed Flow Debugging
expected.text: incidents are hard to reconstruct across many async hops.

cases.referenceAnswer: Synchronous communication is best when the caller needs an immediate answer and the interaction is short and on the critical path, while asynchronous messaging is better when temporal decoupling matters, such as workflows, retries, burst smoothing, and eventual consistency [[Microservices#Communication patterns]]. The tradeoff is that synchronous chains can amplify latency and failure propagation, whereas async systems are harder to reconstruct during incidents because there is no single request thread showing the full workflow [[Microservices#Communication patterns]] [[Event-Driven Architecture#4) Distributed Flow Debugging]].

cases.query: How do microservices organize boundaries and data ownership, and what deployment and governance properties do they aim for?

expected.heading: Core principles
expected.text: Boundaries follow business capabilities

cases.referenceAnswer: Microservices split boundaries by business capabilities such as Orders, Inventory, Billing, and Shipping, so the architecture follows bounded contexts rather than technical layers [[Microservices#Core principles]]. Each service owns its own schema and persistence model, and services should not read each other’s tables directly [[Microservices#Core principles]]. The model also emphasizes independent deployment and decentralized governance, so services can ship, roll back, and scale independently while teams keep local autonomy within shared platform standards [[Microservices#Core principles]].

cases.query: What is the risk of over-modularizing too early in a modular monolith, and when should modules be split?

expected.heading: 3 Over modularization too early
expected.text: teams create many modules before domain boundaries are stable

cases.referenceAnswer: Over-modularizing too early can create constant boundary churn and accidental complexity because teams define many modules before the domain boundaries are stable [[Modular Monolith#3 Over modularization too early]]. The guidance is to start with a few clear bounded contexts and split only when change frequency and ownership data show real pressure [[Modular Monolith#3 Over modularization too early]].

cases.query: If my code is on GitHub and I have no existing CI/CD investment, which CI/CD tool should I start with, and when would Azure DevOps Pipelines or Jenkins be a better choice?

expected.heading: Decision Rule
expected.text: **Start with GitHub Actions** if your code is on GitHub and you have no existing CI/CD investment.

cases.referenceAnswer: Start with GitHub Actions if your code is on GitHub and you have no existing CI/CD investment. Azure DevOps Pipelines is a better fit when deploying to Azure services, needing enterprise approval gates and audit trails, or when your organization already uses Azure DevOps for work tracking. Jenkins is mainly for air-gapped or on-premises environments where cloud CI/CD is not an option, or when you already have a Jenkins investment that is too costly to migrate. [[CI CD tools#Decision Rule]]

cases.query: What causes secrets to leak in CI logs, and what should I do to prevent it?

expected.heading: Secret Leakage in Logs
expected.text: a pipeline step prints environment variables or request bodies to the log, exposing API keys, connection strings, or tokens.

cases.referenceAnswer: Secrets can leak when pipeline steps print environment variables or request bodies to the log, exposing API keys, connection strings, or tokens. This often happens because debugging commands like env, printenv, or verbose HTTP logging are added during troubleshooting and then left in place. To prevent it, use the CI platform’s secret masking, never print environment variables in pipeline steps, and audit pipeline logs before making a repo public. [[CI CD tools#Secret Leakage in Logs]]

cases.query: How should I handle flaky tests that randomly block deployments in CI?

expected.heading: Flaky Tests Blocking Deploys
expected.text: a test that passes 90% of the time fails randomly in CI, blocking the deployment pipeline.

cases.referenceAnswer: If a test randomly blocks deployments, quarantine it immediately by marking it as skipped with a tracking issue. Then fix the root cause instead of repeatedly re-running the pipeline, because that erodes trust in CI. The recommended fixes are to use test containers for external dependencies, mock time-dependent behavior, and isolate shared state between tests. [[CI CD tools#Flaky Tests Blocking Deploys]]

cases.query: For a new service, when should I choose rolling, blue-green, canary, linear, or all-at-once deployment strategies?

expected.heading: Decision Rule
expected.text: **Start with In-Place (Rolling)** for any new service — it's the Kubernetes default, costs nothing extra, and handles most cases.

expected.heading: Comparison
expected.text: Blue-Green | None | Instant (flip) | 2× during deploy | Low | High-availability, payment flows

cases.referenceAnswer: For a new service, start with In-Place (Rolling) because it is the Kubernetes default, costs nothing extra, and handles most cases. Choose Blue-Green when rollback speed is critical or you need to validate the full environment before cutover, and choose Canary when you have more than 10k daily users plus a service mesh or weighted load balancer and automated SLO-based rollback. Use Linear for AWS Lambda deployments, and avoid All-at-Once in production unless downtime is contractually acceptable and infrastructure cost is the main constraint. [[Deployment Strategies#Decision Rule]] [[Deployment Strategies#Comparison]]

cases.query: What is the Red-Green-Refactor cycle in TDD, and how long should each iteration take?

expected.heading: Test-Driven Development
expected.text: Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a development practice where you write a failing test *before* writing the production code that satisfies it.

expected.heading: The Red-Green-Refactor Loop
expected.text: Each iteration should take 2–10 minutes.

cases.referenceAnswer: TDD uses the Red → Green → Refactor cycle: first write a failing test, then write the simplest code to make it pass, and finally refactor while keeping the tests green [[Test-Driven Development]] [[Test-Driven Development#The Red-Green-Refactor Loop]]. Each iteration should take about 2–10 minutes; if it takes longer, the step is probably too large and should be split [[Test-Driven Development#The Red-Green-Refactor Loop]].

cases.query: How does TDD differ from simply aiming for high test coverage?

expected.heading: Test-Driven Development
expected.text: TDD is not primarily about test coverage — it is a design technique.

cases.referenceAnswer: TDD is not mainly about maximizing test coverage; it is a design technique [[Test-Driven Development]]. By writing the test first, you are forced to define the public interface and expected behavior before implementation, which tends to produce smaller, more focused units with clearer contracts [[Test-Driven Development]].

cases.query: In event-driven development, how do in-process events differ from distributed events, and what do producers and consumers do?

expected.heading: Event-Driven Development
expected.text: Producers publish events without knowing who will consume them; consumers subscribe and handle events independently.

cases.referenceAnswer: In event-driven development, producers publish immutable events without knowing who will consume them, and consumers subscribe and handle those events independently [[Event-driven#Event-Driven Development]]. The pattern can be used in-process within a single application, for example with MediatR or an in-memory bus, or distributed across services using a broker like RabbitMQ, Azure Service Bus, or Kafka [[Event-driven#Event-Driven Development]].

cases.query: What is the difference between event-driven architecture and event sourcing?

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Event-driven architecture is a communication style: components publish events and others react asynchronously.

cases.referenceAnswer: Event-driven architecture is a communication style in which components publish events and others react asynchronously [[Event-driven#Questions]]. Event sourcing is a persistence pattern in which an entity’s state is derived by replaying its event history rather than storing current state [[Event-driven#Questions]]. You can use event-driven communication without event sourcing, but event sourcing requires event-driven communication and treats events as the source of truth for state [[Event-driven#Questions]].

cases.query: What is the difference between a unit test and a pure function test, and why do both reduce the need for mocks?

expected.heading: Unit Testing
expected.text: A unit test verifies a small, isolated piece of behavior

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: A pure function has no side effects and is referentially transparent: same inputs always produce the same output.

cases.referenceAnswer: A unit test verifies a small, isolated piece of behavior and replaces external dependencies with test doubles so it exercises only the logic under test [[Unit Testing#Unit Testing]]. A pure function has no side effects and is referentially transparent, so it can be tested by calling it with inputs and asserting outputs, with zero mocks needed [[Functional Programming#Questions]]. Both approaches reduce testing friction by keeping the logic under test isolated from external systems [[Unit Testing#Unit Testing]] [[Functional Programming#Questions]].

cases.query: When should events be published after a database write, and how does the Outbox pattern help if broker publishing can fail?

expected.heading: In-Process Domain Events
expected.text: Publish AFTER save — event reflects committed state

expected.heading: Distributed Events and the Outbox Pattern
expected.text: Publishing to a message broker after a database write introduces a reliability gap: the DB write succeeds but the broker publish fails, leaving the event lost.

expected.heading: Publishing Before Persisting
expected.text: **What goes wrong**: the event is published to the broker before the database transaction commits.

cases.referenceAnswer: Events should be published after the database write has successfully committed, so consumers only see committed state [[Event-driven#In-Process Domain Events]] [[Event-driven#Publishing Before Persisting]]. If publishing to the broker can fail after the database write, the Outbox pattern avoids losing the event by storing it in the same transaction as the domain change [[Event-driven#Distributed Events and the Outbox Pattern]]. A background worker can then read the outbox and publish the event later, retrying until it is acknowledged [[Event-driven#Distributed Events and the Outbox Pattern]].

cases.query: How do stubs and mocks differ in unit tests, and what is an example of each?

expected.heading: Test Doubles: Stubs vs Mocks
expected.text: | Type | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | **Stub** | Returns canned data so the test can proceed | `IOrderRepository` that returns a fixed list | | **Mock** | Verifies interactions — was a method c

cases.referenceAnswer: A stub returns canned data so the test can proceed, while a mock verifies interactions such as whether a method was called with the right arguments [[Unit Testing#Test Doubles: Stubs vs Mocks]]. The section gives an example of a stub by setting up `IOrderRepository` to return a fixed list, and an example of a mock by verifying that `IEmailSender.Send(...)` was called once [[Unit Testing#Test Doubles: Stubs vs Mocks]].

cases.query: What testing and concurrency benefits does immutability provide, and when is a mixed FP style recommended in C#?

expected.heading: Tradeoffs
expected.text: | Approach | Strengths | Weaknesses | When to use | |---|---|---|---| | Pure FP (immutable + pure functions) | Testable, parallelizable, predictable | Verbose in C#, GC pressure at scale | Domain logi

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- What makes a function "pure" and why does purity matter for testing? > - A pure function has no side effects and is referentially transparent: same inputs always produce the same output

cases.referenceAnswer: Pure FP is described as testable, parallelizable, and predictable, while immutability helps concurrent code because immutable objects can be shared freely across threads without locks [[Functional Programming#Tradeoffs]] [[Functional Programming#Questions]]. The recommended style for most production .NET codebases is mixed FP at the boundaries and OOP inside, because it is pragmatic and idiomatic C# [[Functional Programming#Tradeoffs]]. The decision rule is to default to immutable records and LINQ pipelines for domain logic and data transformations, and only switch to mutable structures when profiling shows a real allocation bottleneck [[Functional Programming#Tradeoffs]].
